railway_suffers_8_hour_platform_outage_a.headline: Railway suffers 8-hour platform outage after GCP erroneous account suspension

incident.summary: On May 19-20, 2026, Railway — a cloud deployment platform hosting approximately 10 million services — experienced a platform-wide outage lasting approximately 8 hours after Google Cloud Platform incorrectly suspended Railway's production account via an automated enforcement action with no prior warning. The suspension took Railway's API, control plane, databases, and all GCP-hosted compute offline instantly, and as cached network routing tables expired, the outage cascaded to workloads on Railway's own bare-metal infrastructure and AWS burst-cloud, rendering all regions globally unreachable. Railway's detailed post-mortem was published on May 20-21, 2026 and widely disclosed across technology media within the sweep window, with Railway's founder stating the company spends $2 million per month with GCP and was shut down without notice.
incident.severity_rationale: A complete 8-hour platform-wide outage affecting approximately 10 million services globally, with cascading failure across multi-cloud infrastructure, secondary data loss reports, and significant customer impact warrants severity 4; not a 5 as Railway is a mid-tier platform and no confirmed permanent data loss was reported at scale.

secondary_mappings.why: The outage exposed a critical single-provider architectural dependency: Railway's multi-cloud mesh had a hard dependency on a GCP-hosted routing control plane, meaning a single upstream provider action cascaded to a full platform failure across all regions and cloud environments.

Google Cloud's automated account suspension of Railway — a $2M/month GCP customer with no prior warning — illustrates extreme third-party concentration risk; the incident mirrored a prior GCP-caused UniSuper catastrophe and prompted industry-wide concern over cloud provider automated enforcement.

An 8-hour complete platform outage affecting ~10 million hosted services, with some customer databases reporting data loss and many deployments requiring manual intervention post-recovery, constitutes a significant BCN event for Railway and its downstream customers.

Railway's CEO publicly expressed shock and users threatened to migrate platforms, with community posts citing repeated recent instability; the incident materially damaged stakeholder confidence in Railway's reliability as a production deployment platform.

control_objectives.statement: Ensure Railway's production control-plane components — specifically the GCP-hosted CloudSQL database and API — cannot be rendered unavailable by a single upstream provider's automated enforcement action, eliminating the routing-cache expiry cascade that blacked out all regions on May 19-20, 2026.

Maintain infrastructure resilience such that Railway's bare-metal (Metal) and AWS burst-cloud workloads continue to serve traffic even when the GCP-hosted control plane is suspended, by distributing routing-table state independently of any single cloud account.

Establish governance over Railway's third-party cloud dependencies — specifically GCP and GitHub — to ensure contractual protections against unilateral automated account suspension and rate-limiting that can cascade into platform-wide customer outages.

Maintain a tested business-continuity capability that preserves Railway API and routing-plane function for at least 8 hours following loss of the primary GCP production account, preventing the May 2026 all-regions blackout scenario from recurring.

Ensure Railway's incident-response process provides real-time, accurate customer communication during a platform-wide outage originating from a third-party provider action, including escalation paths that do not depend on GCP-hosted systems.

Protect Railway's commercial reputation by demonstrating, via published architectural changes following the May 2026 postmortem, that the GCP single-point-of-failure has been structurally eliminated and that repeat outage risk is materially reduced.

master_controls.statement: Railway must remove GCP from the routing data plane's 'hot path' by Q3 2026 as committed in the May 21 postmortem, ensuring Metal and AWS edge proxies maintain independent routing tables not seeded from the GCP-hosted control-plane API.

Railway must implement automated detection of GCP account-suspension events — distinct from normal API error signals — so that the edge routing layer can fail-over to a cached or independently stored routing state before TTL expiry causes global 404 propagation.

Railway must distribute CloudSQL read replicas and the control-plane API across at least one non-GCP provider so that an erroneous GCP account suspension, as occurred on May 19 2026, cannot simultaneously offline the database and the API.

Railway must harden the edge proxy routing-table cache TTL so that Metal and AWS workloads continue to resolve routes for a minimum defined window (e.g. 4 hours) following loss of the GCP-hosted network control plane, preventing the immediate 404 cascade observed on May 19 2026.

Railway must implement real-time monitoring that alerts on GCP account-status changes — including suspension events — within 60 seconds, separate from application-level health checks that themselves depend on the GCP-hosted API.

Railway must contractually require GCP to provide a minimum 24-hour human-reviewed notice before any automated enforcement action on accounts with monthly spend exceeding $500K, consistent with the $2M/month spend level Railway disclosed on May 20 2026.

Railway must conduct a structured concentration-risk review of its GCP dependency — which as of May 19 2026 hosted the API, CloudSQL, and network control plane simultaneously — and document a time-bound plan to reduce single-provider criticality below a defined threshold.

Railway must establish a dedicated GCP account-recovery runbook, including pre-authorized escalation contacts at Google, so that an erroneous suspension (as on May 19 2026) can be reversed in under 2 hours rather than the approximately 8 hours experienced.

Railway must maintain a business-continuity plan specifically addressing loss of the GCP production account, with a documented RTO for API and routing-plane recovery that does not require GCP account reinstatement as a prerequisite.

Railway must conduct a tabletop exercise simulating an unannounced GCP account suspension within 90 days of the May 2026 incident, validating that the newly decoupled routing architecture prevents all-regions blackout before the planned Q3 2026 migration completes.

Railway must publish a post-incident customer communication within 2 hours of any future platform-wide outage onset, with status-page updates independent of GCP-hosted infrastructure, given that the May 19 2026 outage took GCP-dependent communication channels offline simultaneously.

Railway must publish a dated architectural-change commitment within 30 days of the May 21 2026 postmortem — including removal of GCP from the data-plane hot path — to provide enterprise and paying customers evidence that the structural failure mode has been closed.

Railway's CEO and founder must provide a direct stakeholder communication to enterprise customers — distinct from the public postmortem — acknowledging the five major incidents between November 2025 and May 2026 and presenting the architectural remediation roadmap with quarterly milestones.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must remove GCP from the routing data
adaptive_controls.statement: By September 30, 2026, Railway's infrastructure engineering team must complete the removal of GCP from the data-plane routing hot path as committed in the May 21, 2026 postmortem, ensuring that Railway Metal edge proxies and AWS burst-cloud nodes maintain routing tables seeded exclusively from a non-GCP control-plane source — not from the GCP-hosted API that was taken offline on May 19, 2026. Completion evidence must include: (a) a dated architectural diagram showing the revised routing-table propagation path with GCP designated as secondary/failover only, (b) a live traffic report confirming zero dependency on GCP for primary route resolution during a 72-hour validation window, and (c) sign-off from Railway's VP/Head of Infrastructure. The accountable owner is Railway's CTO or equivalent infrastructure lead.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires GCP hot-path removal; this adaptation binds it to Railway's own May 21 postmortem commitment, specifies that the routing-table seed source — the precise mechanism by which the May 19 cascade reached Metal and AWS after GCP went offline — must be independently sourced, and sets the Q3 2026 deadline Railway publicly stated.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must implement automated detection of GCP account-suspension
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway's platform engineering team must implement, by August 31, 2026, an out-of-band GCP account-status detection mechanism — operating independently of the GCP-hosted API and CloudSQL endpoints that were suspended on May 19, 2026 — that distinguishes an account-suspension HTTP response or GCP Admin SDK suspension event from transient API errors, and upon detection immediately instructs Railway Metal and AWS edge proxies to lock their current routing-table snapshot and cease TTL-based expiry for a minimum 4-hour hold window. The detection system must itself be hosted outside GCP, and automated alerting must fire within 60 seconds of a confirmed suspension signal to Railway's on-call SRE. Evidence of completion requires: a runbook documenting the detection logic and failover trigger, results of a simulated suspension test showing sub-60-second alert and successful routing-table lock, and sign-off from Railway's Head of SRE.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires suspension-event detection distinct from normal API errors; this adaptation anchors the requirement to the specific failure mode of May 19 — where routing-table TTL expiry after GCP went offline cascaded to Metal and AWS — and mandates the detection system itself be GCP-independent, closing the circular dependency that made the outage undetectable until customer impact was total.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must distribute CloudSQL read replicas and the
adaptive_controls.statement: By October 31, 2026, Railway must deploy at least one fully synchronised read replica of its CloudSQL production database and a warm standby instance of the Railway control-plane API on a non-GCP provider (Railway Metal bare-metal or AWS), such that either component can independently serve traffic within 10 minutes of a GCP account suspension without requiring GCP account reinstatement — directly addressing the May 19, 2026 failure in which the simultaneous loss of the GCP-hosted API and CloudSQL database left no surviving control-plane path. The accountable owner is Railway's CTO; evidence of completion requires: (a) a tested failover procedure demonstrating API and database continuity during a simulated GCP suspension, (b) replication lag metrics showing the non-GCP replica is within a defined RPO (not to exceed 5 minutes), and (c) an updated architecture document confirming no single-provider control-plane dependency.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires multi-provider database and API distribution; this adaptation specifies the exact Railway systems — CloudSQL and the control-plane API — that were simultaneously suspended on May 19, pins the failover time objective to the need to avoid the 8-hour outage duration, and requires the non-GCP instance to be warm rather than cold-standby, reflecting that a GCP suspension removes the ability to promote a GCP-hosted replica.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must harden the edge proxy routing-table cache
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway's infrastructure engineering team must reconfigure the routing-table cache TTL on all Railway Metal edge proxies and AWS burst-cloud nodes so that cached routes remain valid and resolvable for a minimum of 4 hours following loss of connectivity to the GCP-hosted network control plane, preventing the immediate 404 propagation cascade observed on May 19, 2026 as expired TTLs rendered all global regions unreachable. This change must be deployed to production by July 31, 2026 — ahead of the broader Q3 GCP hot-path removal — and validated by a controlled test in which the GCP control-plane connection is severed in a staging environment and routing continuity is confirmed for the full 4-hour hold window. Evidence of completion: updated proxy configuration files with TTL values documented, staging test results signed off by Railway's Head of Infrastructure, and a monitoring alert confirming any TTL regression below the 4-hour threshold.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires TTL hardening with a minimum survivability window; this adaptation ties the requirement directly to the May 19 cascade mechanism — expiring routing-table TTLs that propagated 404 errors globally — specifies the 4-hour floor Railway named in its postmortem planning, and requires the fix to land before Q3 migration as an interim safeguard.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must implement real-time monitoring that alerts on
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway's SRE team must deploy, by July 31, 2026, a real-time GCP account-status monitor that polls the GCP Resource Manager or Admin SDK from an infrastructure node outside GCP (Railway Metal or AWS) and alerts Railway's on-call engineer within 60 seconds of detecting an account suspension, billing hold, or enforcement-action status change — operating entirely independently of the GCP-hosted Railway API and CloudSQL that were taken offline simultaneously on May 19, 2026 and thus could not generate application-level health alerts. The monitor must be integrated into Railway's existing incident-management paging workflow (not a standalone dashboard), and its availability must itself be subject to a separate uptime check hosted outside GCP. Completion evidence: monitor deployed and producing alerts in production, a test-run log showing sub-60-second alert latency for a synthetic suspension event, and quarterly review by Railway's Head of SRE confirming continued GCP-independence of the monitoring path.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires account-status alerting separate from application health checks; this adaptation specifies that the monitoring path must be physically hosted outside GCP — closing the circular dependency where Railway's own GCP-hosted API was the system that would normally have generated alerts, making the outage initially invisible through normal monitoring channels.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must contractually require GCP to provide a
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway's legal and commercial team, led by its CEO/founder Jake Cooper, must initiate contract renegotiation with Google Cloud Platform within 60 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem to secure a contractual commitment requiring a minimum 24-hour human-reviewed notice period before any automated enforcement or account-suspension action is applied to Railway's GCP account — given that Railway's reported $2 million/month GCP spend places it among Google's significant enterprise customers and the May 19, 2026 suspension was applied with no prior outreach whatsoever. If GCP declines to provide such a commitment contractually, Railway must document this outcome and incorporate the absence of contractual protection as a named risk driver accelerating the Q3 2026 architectural remediation timeline. Evidence of completion: a signed contract amendment or a dated written GCP response, plus Railway's CEO sign-off on either the secured commitment or the documented risk escalation.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires contractual notice protections; this adaptation anchors the obligation to Railway's specific disclosed spend level of $2M/month, names Jake Cooper as the accountable executive, and introduces a mandatory documented fallback — escalating the architecture timeline — if GCP refuses, preventing the control from becoming a dead letter if negotiations fail.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must conduct a structured concentration-risk review of
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 45 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem, Railway's CTO must complete and publish internally a structured single-provider concentration-risk assessment documenting the full scope of Railway's GCP dependency as it existed on May 19, 2026 — specifically the simultaneous hosting of the production API, CloudSQL database, and network control plane on GCP — rating each component against a defined criticality threshold, and producing a time-bound remediation plan with quarterly milestones through Q4 2026 that reduces no single provider from hosting more than one tier-1 control-plane component simultaneously. The assessment must be reviewed by Railway's executive leadership team and shared with the board or equivalent governing body. Evidence of completion: a dated assessment document, a milestone roadmap with named owners per component, and documented sign-off from Railway's CEO.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires a concentration-risk review with a time-bound plan; this adaptation specifies the exact three-component GCP concentration — API, CloudSQL, and network control plane — that made the May 19 suspension a total-outage event rather than a partial one, and sets a concrete single-provider rule (no more than one tier-1 component per provider) as the measurable threshold the review must produce.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must establish a dedicated GCP account-recovery runbook
adaptive_controls.statement: By June 30, 2026, Railway must establish and document a GCP account-suspension recovery runbook that includes: (a) pre-authorised named escalation contacts within Google Cloud's enterprise support and trust-and-safety organisations, obtained and verified before any future suspension event; (b) a step-by-step escalation procedure with time-boxed actions targeting GCP account reinstatement within 2 hours of suspension detection — compared to the approximately 8 hours experienced on May 19, 2026; and (c) a parallel track for customer communication and partial-service restoration that does not wait for GCP reinstatement. The runbook must be tested in a tabletop scenario within 90 days of the May 2026 incident and reviewed quarterly thereafter. The accountable owner is Railway's Head of SRE, with CEO sign-off on the Google escalation contacts.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires a recovery runbook with pre-authorised escalation contacts; this adaptation sets the 2-hour reinstatement target in explicit contrast to the 8-hour actual recovery on May 19, requires Google contacts to be verified in advance rather than sought during an active suspension, and mandates a parallel restoration track — reflecting that waiting for GCP reinstatement as the sole recovery path was the structural bottleneck on May 19.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must maintain a business-continuity plan specifically addressing
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway's CTO must document and maintain a business-continuity plan, completed by August 31, 2026, that specifically addresses total loss of Railway's GCP production account — including simultaneous loss of the production API, CloudSQL database, and GCP-hosted compute as occurred on May 19, 2026 — and defines an RTO for API and routing-plane recovery that is achievable without GCP account reinstatement, using Railway Metal and/or AWS as the sole recovery substrate. The plan must specify the recovery sequence, the data source for routing-table reconstruction, the database failover target, and the maximum acceptable customer-facing outage duration, and must not assume any GCP-dependent communication or monitoring channel is available during execution. The plan must be reviewed and approved by Railway's CEO and tested as part of the tabletop exercise required under MC-OPS-008.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires a BCP addressing GCP account loss with a GCP-independent RTO; this adaptation specifies that the plan must cover the exact concurrent failure mode of May 19 — API, database, and compute offline simultaneously — and explicitly prohibits GCP-dependent channels from appearing anywhere in the recovery sequence, closing the gap where Railway's recovery on May 19 was gated on GCP reinstatement rather than on independent restoration.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must conduct a tabletop exercise simulating an
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 90 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem (by approximately August 19, 2026), Railway's SRE and infrastructure teams must conduct a structured tabletop exercise simulating an unannounced, instantaneous GCP account suspension equivalent to the May 19, 2026 event — specifically testing whether the revised routing architecture (with hardened TTLs and non-GCP control-plane seeding) prevents global 404 propagation to Railway Metal and AWS workloads without GCP reinstatement. The exercise must include a GitHub rate-limiting scenario as a secondary stressor, reflecting the actual compound failure observed during recovery on May 19-20, 2026. A written after-action report identifying any remaining single points of failure must be delivered to Railway's CTO within 5 business days, and any critical gaps must be remediated before the Q3 2026 GCP hot-path removal is declared complete.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires a tabletop exercise validating the decoupled architecture; this adaptation scripts the exercise around the specific May 19 failure sequence — instantaneous suspension, TTL cascade, and the secondary GitHub rate-limiting event that compounded recovery — ensuring the exercise tests the actual failure modes rather than a generic cloud-provider outage scenario.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must publish a post-incident customer communication within
adaptive_controls.statement: Railway must deploy, by July 15, 2026, a status-page and customer-communication infrastructure that is hosted entirely outside GCP — on Railway Metal or a third-party provider — capable of publishing a customer-visible incident notification within 2 hours of any future platform-wide outage onset, even if the GCP-hosted Railway API and CloudSQL are simultaneously offline as they were on May 19, 2026 at 22:20 UTC. The system must include automated incident-detection triggers that do not depend on GCP-hosted monitoring agents, a templated initial-communication workflow executable by any on-call SRE without access to GCP systems, and a tested publishing path verified to function during a simulated total GCP account suspension. Evidence of completion: architecture diagram confirming no GCP dependency, a test-publish log from a GCP-isolated environment, and sign-off from Railway's Head of SRE.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires GCP-independent status communications within 2 hours; this adaptation specifies that both the detection trigger and the publishing path must function under total GCP account suspension — the exact condition of May 19 — preventing the scenario where communication channels are taken offline at the same moment as the services they are meant to report on.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway must publish a dated architectural-change commitment within
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem (by June 20, 2026), Railway's CEO Jake Cooper must publish a dated, public architectural-change commitment — distinct from the postmortem narrative — that specifies: (a) the Q3 2026 target date for removing GCP from the data-plane routing hot path; (b) the interim milestones for CloudSQL replica distribution and control-plane API migration to non-GCP infrastructure; and (c) the monitoring and TTL-hardening changes being deployed as near-term safeguards. The commitment document must be linked from Railway's status page and postmortem, formatted for enterprise and paying customers to evaluate as evidence of structural remediation, and must be updated with completion dates as each milestone is achieved. Accountability rests with Railway's CEO; evidence of publication is the dated, publicly accessible document URL.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires a dated architectural-change commitment within 30 days; this adaptation specifies that the document must be structured as a customer-facing evidence artifact — not just an engineering blog post — and must enumerate the specific Railway systems (GCP hot path, CloudSQL, control-plane API) that failed on May 19, giving enterprise customers a concrete checklist against which to measure Railway's remediation progress.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Railway's CEO and founder must provide a direct
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem, Railway's CEO Jake Cooper must deliver a direct written communication to Railway's enterprise and paying customers — separate from the public postmortem and distinct from status-page updates — that explicitly acknowledges the pattern of at least five major incidents between November 2025 and May 2026, names the May 19, 2026 GCP account suspension as the most severe due to the simultaneous loss of the API, CloudSQL, and routing control plane, and presents a quarterly remediation roadmap through Q4 2026 with named milestone owners and measurable completion criteria for each architectural change. The communication must be delivered via a channel (direct email or customer portal message) that does not route through GCP-hosted Railway infrastructure. Evidence of completion: dated delivery confirmation to the enterprise customer list and a copy of the communication retained for compliance purposes, reviewed by Railway's CEO.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control requires direct stakeholder communication acknowledging the multi-incident pattern; this adaptation requires Jake Cooper specifically — who made the $2M/month disclosure publicly — to address enterprise customers through a GCP-independent channel, and frames the communication around the five-incident pattern rather than treating May 19 as an isolated event, reflecting the reputational context that enterprise customers evaluating Railway's reliability face.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Datadog
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Datadog External Provider Status
risk_coverage_vendors.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Datadog is an observability and security platform that provides real-time monitoring of cloud infrastructure and third-party providers. Its External Provider Status feature detects third-party cloud provider degradations before they are confirmed by vendor status pages, using aggregated APM telemetry across thousands of customers.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Datadog External Provider Status monitors the real-time health of 30+ SaaS APIs and AWS services and uses a Bayesian detection model to flag provider degradations faster than vendor status pages — directly supporting MC-TEC-003's requirement for real-time monitoring of GCP account-status changes independent of the GCP-hosted API. However, the October 2025 release covers 13 AWS services and 30+ SaaS APIs but does not yet list GCP account-suspension events among documented monitored providers, so this is a partial fit: the detection mechanism is architecturally correct but coverage of a GCP account-suspension event specifically requires confirmation.

capability_claims.claim: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers, detects degradations faster than vendor status pages, and uses aggregated APM telemetry data to flag provider outages ahead of official confirmation.
capability_claims.source_span: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers, including 13 AWS services across global regions and widely used SaaS APIs such as GitHub, Stripe, and OpenAI.

capability_claims.claim: Datadog External Provider Status detects provider outages before they are confirmed by vendors, as demonstrated by flagging a DynamoDB degradation 32 minutes before AWS acknowledged it on their status page.
capability_claims.source_span: Datadog automatically monitors APM telemetry data across thousands of customers for errors tied to external providers. By aggregating traffic across organizations,

capability_claims.claim: Datadog External Provider Status allows teams to configure monitors to alert when provider degradations are detected, with options to alert for all detected provider issues or only when degradations directly affect specific services, integrating with Slack, PagerDuty, and Microsoft Teams.
capability_claims.source_span: You can configure monitors to alert your teams when provider degradations are detected. Options include: Alerts for all detected provider issues; Alerts only when degradations directly affect your services.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Datadog External Provider Status operates entirely independently of the monitored infrastructure — it uses Datadog's own globally distributed APM data pipeline to detect degradations by observing aggregated error rates across thousands of customer environments. When GCP exhibits anomalous error patterns consistent with a service suspension, Datadog's Bayesian detection model surfaces the degradation before the provider confirms it. The customer can configure a Datadog monitor to page the on-call SRE within seconds of a detected degradation, satisfying the 60-second alerting requirement in MC-TEC-003. Crucially, since Datadog's own infrastructure is independent of GCP, this monitoring path survives a total GCP account suspension — the very failure mode that affected Railway on May 19, 2026.
grounding_spans: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers, including 13 AWS services across global regions and widely used SaaS APIs such as GitHub, Stripe, and OpenAI.

Datadog automatically monitors APM telemetry data across thousands of customers for errors tied to external providers. By aggregating traffic across organizations, our Bayesian detection model identifies abnormal error rates and confirms when multiple customers are affected.
You can configure monitors to alert your teams when provider degradations are detected.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides a single real-time dashboard showing the health of 40+ third-party providers, with a historical view of up to 90 days of degradation history for resilience planning.
enabling_features.source_span: The External Provider Status page offers a single dashboard for monitoring the near real-time health of 30+ SaaS APIs... and 13 AWS services... across regions. The External Provider Status page shows both the live view and a historical view that provides up to 90 days of degradation history.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Maps detected provider degradations directly to the customer's own APM services, tracing the disruption through the dependency chain to show the true scope of impact.
enabling_features.source_span: External Provider Status maps detected degradations directly to your APM services, tracing the disruption through your dependency chain. This avoids the common mistake of flagging only the direct integration and instead surfaces the true scope of impact across your architecture.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Allows customers to configure monitors that fire within seconds of detecting a provider degradation, with flexible scoping (all provider issues vs. only those affecting the customer's own services).
enabling_features.source_span: You can configure monitors to alert your teams when provider degradations are detected. Options include: Alerts for all detected provider issues; Alerts only when degradations directly affect your services.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Integrate Railway's application with Datadog APM, hosted on Railway Metal or AWS infrastructure independent of GCP.
2. Configure a Datadog External Provider Status monitor scoped to GCP (or the GCP-adjacent API patterns) to alert Railway's on-call SRE.
3. Set the monitor alerting threshold to fire within 60 seconds of detecting a confirmed GCP degradation, routing the alert to Railway's PagerDuty or Slack on-call channel.
4. Link the GCP External Provider Status monitor to Railway's incident-management workflow so that a GCP suspension auto-creates an incident and triggers the GCP account-suspension recovery runbook (MC-TPR-023).
5. Review the 90-day degradation history quarterly to identify recurring GCP reliability patterns and inform the concentration-risk assessment required under MC-TPR-022.
grounding_spans: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers
You can configure monitors to alert your teams when provider degradations are detected.
Notifications integrate with Datadog's ecosystem, including Slack, PagerDuty, and Microsoft Teams.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Real-time alerting on GCP account-status changes within 60 seconds
dimension_coverage.note: Datadog's detection is faster than vendor status pages (demonstrated at 32 minutes ahead for AWS), but the exact latency for a GCP account-suspension event (as opposed to a service degradation) is not documented. The 60-second paging requirement in MC-TEC-003 may require a synthetic monitor complement.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers

dimension_coverage.dimension: Detection mechanism independent of GCP-hosted Railway API
dimension_coverage.note: Datadog's detection infrastructure operates independently of the customer's monitored cloud provider — it uses Datadog's own globally distributed data pipeline, not the GCP-hosted API.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Datadog automatically monitors APM telemetry data across thousands of customers for errors tied to external providers. By aggregating traffic across organizations, our Bayesian detection model identifies abnormal error rates

dimension_coverage.dimension: Distinguishing account-suspension from transient API errors
dimension_coverage.note: Datadog detects provider-level degradation patterns but the page does not document a specific signal that distinguishes an account suspension (a GCP enforcement action) from a general service degradation.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: It detects degradations faster than vendor status pages, maps them directly to your APM services, and traces their effects across your architecture's dependency chain.

mitigation_proof.outcome: Datadog flagged a DynamoDB degradation 32 minutes before AWS acknowledged it on their status page, demonstrating early detection ahead of official confirmation.
mitigation_proof.source_span: during a DynamoDB degradation on July 3, 2025, Datadog surfaced the issue 32 minutes before AWS acknowledged it on their status page.

mitigation_limits.limit: Datadog External Provider Status does not document specific monitoring of GCP account-suspension enforcement events as a named provider signal. Coverage for GCP is not explicitly listed alongside the 13 named AWS services.
grounding_spans: Datadog External Provider Status provides real-time visibility into the health of more than 40 third-party providers, including 13 AWS services across global regions and widely used SaaS APIs such as GitHub, Stripe, and OpenAI.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Datadog's External Provider Status addresses third-party degradation detection (adjacent coverage) but the Railway incident was an account suspension — an enforcement/policy action, not a service degradation — making the vendor's core capability only tangentially relevant to the actual failure mode, with evidence drawn from product pages rather than named customer case studies.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: IBM NS1 Connect
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: IBM NS1 Connect – Intelligent DNS Traffic Steering with Filter Chain Failover
risk_coverage_vendors.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: IBM NS1 Connect transforms DNS into a real-time traffic control plane that automatically reroutes traffic between infrastructure providers using health-check-driven filter chains, enabling active-active and active-passive multi-cloud failover independent of any single cloud provider.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: IBM NS1 Connect's filter chain technology uses DNS health monitoring to automatically reroute traffic between providers when one goes down — directly addressing MC-TEC-001's requirement to remove GCP from the routing data plane hot path by ensuring Railway Metal and AWS edge proxies can receive traffic via DNS-level steering independent of the GCP control plane. The filter chain 'Up' monitor detects endpoint unavailability and the 'Priority' filter automatically redirects to secondary providers, which maps precisely to MC-TEC-012's automated failover-before-TTL-expiry requirement.

capability_claims.claim: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption, monitors network status, and switches endpoints as needed.
capability_claims.source_span: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain™ technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption.

capability_claims.claim: IBM NS1 Connect active-passive failover uses an 'Up' filter driven by NS1 or third-party monitoring data to detect a primary provider outage and automatically route traffic to secondary systems, creating a logic that prioritizes active systems over passive ones.
capability_claims.source_span: As in the active-active use case, NS1 or third-party data sources monitor the status of your application delivery infrastructure and route traffic to secondary systems in the event of a primary system outage.

capability_claims.claim: IBM NS1 Connect automatically prevents downtime by combining real-time health checks with intelligent traffic steering to keep applications available without manual intervention.
capability_claims.source_span: Automatically prevent downtime: Combine real-time health checks with intelligent traffic steering to keep applications available without manual intervention.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: IBM NS1 Connect operates as a DNS-layer traffic control plane completely independent of any single cloud provider. For Railway's use case, NS1 would be configured with an active-passive filter chain where GCP endpoints are designated as primary and Railway Metal and AWS are designated as passive failover endpoints. NS1's health monitoring continuously checks the availability of the GCP-hosted API endpoint. When GCP's endpoint becomes unreachable — as it did on May 19, 2026 — the filter chain's 'Up' filter immediately detects the failure and the 'Priority' filter automatically redirects all DNS queries to Railway Metal or AWS endpoints, routing traffic away from GCP before TTL expiry can cause a global 404 cascade. This automated DNS-level failover directly prevents the routing-cache expiry cascade that blacked out all Railway regions.
grounding_spans: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain™ technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption.
NS1 or third-party data sources monitor the status of your application delivery infrastructure and route traffic to secondary systems in the event of a primary system outage.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Configures DNS routing rules so that when the primary provider (GCP) is detected as down by the 'Up' filter, traffic is automatically redirected to secondary providers (Railway Metal, AWS) via the 'Priority' filter without manual intervention.
enabling_features.source_span: As in the active-active use case, NS1 or third-party data sources monitor the status of your application delivery infrastructure and route traffic to secondary systems in the event of a primary system outage.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Continuously monitors endpoint health and automatically routes traffic to healthy destinations, preventing outages before users are impacted without manual intervention.
enabling_features.source_span: Keep applications available by continuously monitoring endpoint health and automatically routing traffic to healthy destinations. NS1 helps you prevent outages before users are impacted—without manual intervention.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Directs users based on real-time performance, availability, and geography across multiple cloud providers, enabling Railway to maintain a routing layer independent of any single provider.
enabling_features.source_span: IBM® NS1 Connect® transforms DNS into a decision layer that routes users to the best endpoint based on real-time performance, availability and geography.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Configure NS1 Connect zones for Railway's primary API and routing plane endpoints, designating GCP as primary and Railway Metal and AWS as passive failover targets.
2. Enable NS1 health monitoring on the GCP-hosted Railway API endpoint, with the 'Up' filter configured to detect endpoint unavailability.
3. Configure the 'Priority' filter chain to automatically redirect traffic to Railway Metal endpoints when GCP is flagged as down, without requiring manual intervention.
4. Set NS1's DNS TTL to a value that ensures failover completes before the edge routing cache TTL expires (supporting MC-TEC-017).
5. Test the failover configuration in staging by simulating a GCP endpoint blackhole, confirming that NS1 routes to Railway Metal within the required window.
6. Incorporate NS1 failover into the GCP account-suspension recovery runbook (MC-TPR-023) as the primary automated response mechanism.
grounding_spans: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain™ technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption.
You set the rules and the priorities upfront; everything after that happens automatically.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Removing GCP from the routing data plane hot path
dimension_coverage.note: NS1 can route all traffic to Railway Metal or AWS at the DNS level, making GCP a secondary-only target. This directly implements the MC-TEC-001 requirement.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain™ technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Automated detection and failover before routing-cache TTL expiry
dimension_coverage.note: NS1's filter chain detects the GCP endpoint outage via the 'Up' filter and automatically redirects traffic to Metal/AWS before TTL expiry causes global 404 propagation.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: NS1 or third-party data sources monitor the status of your application delivery infrastructure and route traffic to secondary systems in the event of a primary system outage.

mitigation_limits.limit: IBM NS1 Connect provides DNS-level failover but does not address the CloudSQL database replication requirement of MC-TEC-016; database failover to a non-GCP replica must be handled separately.
grounding_spans: IBM NS1 Connect Filter Chain™ technology uses the power of DNS to automatically reroute traffic between service providers when there is a network service disruption.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: NS1 Connect's DNS-layer traffic rerouting and active-passive failover capabilities map precisely to the multi-cloud provider independence required by this incident, but scores are tempered by evidence resting entirely on product-page marketing claims with no named customer case studies, and IBM's brand weight introduces meaningful reputation-halo risk relative to the capability-led evidence base.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Gremlin
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Gremlin Chaos Engineering Platform – Blackhole Attack and Disaster Recovery FireDrills
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Gremlin is an enterprise chaos engineering platform that enables teams to simulate real-world infrastructure failures — including network blackhole attacks that drop all traffic to specific cloud provider endpoints — to validate disaster recovery plans and business continuity procedures in a safe, controlled, and reversible way.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Gremlin's Blackhole attack drops all network traffic to specific cloud provider hostnames or IP ranges — exactly simulating a GCP account suspension that severs Railway's connection to its GCP-hosted API and CloudSQL. The platform is specifically documented for validating disaster recovery plans via FireDrills and Scenarios, directly mapping to MC-OPS-008's requirement for a tabletop exercise simulating an unannounced GCP account suspension. Gremlin itself is hosted outside of the customer's infrastructure (api.gremlin.com is excluded from blackhole attacks by design), ensuring the chaos control plane survives a total GCP blackout.

capability_claims.claim: Gremlin's Blackhole attack blocks network traffic by dropping IP packets at the transport layer, and can be configured to target specific hostnames, IP addresses, and external service providers — including pre-defined third-party cloud provider regions.
capability_claims.source_span: The Blackhole attack works by blocking network traffic from a host or container. It drops Internet Protocol (IP) packets at the transport layer by using traffic policing features built into the Linux Kernel and the Windows Filtering Platform for

capability_claims.claim: Gremlin's chaos engineering platform can be used to simulate a black swan event such as a third-party provider outage, allowing teams to test their disaster recovery plan and response procedures in a controlled scenario using FireDrills and Scenarios.
capability_claims.source_span: In the context of disaster recovery, we can use Chaos Engineering to recreate or simulate a black swan event. This gives us the opportunity to test our DRP and our response procedures in a controlled scenario,

capability_claims.claim: Gremlin identifies third-party provider outages as a named black swan event category requiring disaster recovery planning, stating that identifying potential threats includes 'Third-party provider outages (i.e. loss of infrastructure owned by a third-party cloud provider)'.
capability_claims.source_span: Third-party provider outages (i.e. loss of infrastructure owned by a third-party cloud provider).

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Gremlin's Blackhole attack targeted at GCP hostnames (e.g., sqladmin.googleapis.com and the Railway production API's GCP endpoint) will drop all network traffic between Railway Metal and AWS nodes and GCP, simulating the exact failure mode that occurred on May 19, 2026. This validates whether the hardened routing-table TTL (MC-TEC-017), the non-GCP control-plane seeding (MC-TEC-001), and the non-GCP CloudSQL read replica (MC-TEC-016) together prevent global 404 propagation without GCP reinstatement. Gremlin's Scenarios feature allows the team to stage the attack with an increasing blast radius (first one hub, then all regions), and the built-in fail-safe halts the attack if the Gremlin agent loses connectivity, providing a safety net for a production-grade FireDrill.
grounding_spans: The Blackhole attack works by blocking network traffic from a host or container.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Drops all network traffic to specified hostnames, IP ranges, or pre-defined cloud provider endpoints, simulating a complete provider account suspension from the perspective of Railway's Metal and AWS nodes.
enabling_features.source_span: Gremlin also provides a list of pre-defined third-party services that you can select for easy targeting, such as an AWS EC2 region.

enabling_features.feature: Scenarios for Staged Blast Radius Expansion
enabling_features.what_it_does: Allows multiple attacks to be chained sequentially with increasing blast radius and magnitude, replicating the cascading nature of a GCP account suspension that progressively takes all regions offline.
enabling_features.source_span: Scenarios let us accurately simulate real-world disaster conditions by incrementally increasing both the blast radius and magnitude of an attack.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Enables teams to run planned FireDrill events that deliberately induce failures to test disaster recovery procedures and runbooks, producing RTO and RPA metrics for post-mortem review.
enabling_features.source_span: We can test our DRP in a much less risky manner by using Chaos Engineering to recreate conditions that would be present during a disaster—such as server outages and network failures—without causing permanent or irreversible damage to our systems.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Within 90 days of the May 21, 2026 postmortem (by August 19, 2026), schedule the MC-OPS-008 tabletop exercise using Gremlin Scenarios.
2. Deploy the Gremlin agent on Railway Metal and AWS burst-cloud nodes (not on GCP nodes), ensuring the chaos control plane survives a GCP blackout.
3. Create a Scenario that targets all GCP hostnames used by Railway's API and CloudSQL (e.g., sqladmin.googleapis.com, the GCP API endpoints) with a Blackhole attack.
4. Stage the Scenario with an increasing blast radius: first a single GCP endpoint, then all GCP endpoints simultaneously, replicating the May 19, 2026 event.
5. Add a GitHub rate-limiting stressor as a secondary Scenario stage, reflecting the compound failure observed during recovery on May 19-20, 2026.
6. Track TTD, TTE, and RTA metrics throughout the FireDrill and compare against the RTO defined in the BCP (MC-OPS-002).
7. Deliver an after-action report to Railway's CTO within 5 business days, identifying any remaining single points of failure before the Q3 2026 GCP hot-path removal.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Simulating unannounced GCP account suspension to validate decoupled routing architecture
dimension_coverage.note: Gremlin's Blackhole attack targeted at GCP endpoints precisely simulates the May 19, 2026 failure mode and validates whether the revised architecture prevents all-regions blackout.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: The Blackhole attack works by blocking network traffic from a host or container. It drops Internet Protocol (IP) packets at the transport layer

mitigation_proof.outcome: Qualtrics used Gremlin to test large-scale disaster recovery activities across 40+ teams independently, saving over 500 engineering hours and reducing per-dependency test times from several hours to four minutes.
mitigation_proof.source_span: Using Gremlin, over 40 of their teams independently planned for failover, which saved over 500 engineering hours and reduced their per-dependency test times from several hours to just four minutes.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Gremlin's verified capabilities map closely to third-party cloud provider outage simulation (exactly the Railway scenario type) and the claimed MCs align with chaos-engineering-based DR validation, but evidence rests on product documentation and marketing copy rather than named customer case studies, and the incident's root cause (erroneous account suspension) is an administrative/policy failure that Gremlin's network-layer blackhole simulation approximates but does not precisely replicate.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Atlassian Statuspage
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Atlassian Statuspage
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Atlassian Statuspage is a hosted incident communication tool that enables companies to inform users about outages and scheduled maintenance via a publicly accessible status page, with subscriber notifications sent by Atlassian's own infrastructure independent of the company's own systems.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Atlassian Statuspage is hosted entirely on Atlassian's own infrastructure, not on Railway's GCP-hosted systems. This means it remains operational even when Railway's GCP-hosted API and CloudSQL are simultaneously offline — directly addressing MC-OPS-018's requirement for a status page and customer-communication infrastructure independent of GCP. The service has explicit documented capability for email and SMS notifications sent by Atlassian's own infrastructure, meeting the 2-hour customer-communication requirement.

capability_claims.claim: Statuspage is a communication tool that helps companies inform their users about outages and scheduled maintenance, with users able to subscribe to updates via email or text messages when an incident occurs.
capability_claims.source_span: Statuspage is a communication tool that helps you inform your users about outages and scheduled maintenance. Your users can subscribe to updates via email or text messages when you have an incident,

capability_claims.claim: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of the customer's own websites or servers, meaning it operates entirely independently of the customer's infrastructure and remains available even when the customer's own systems are offline.
capability_claims.source_span: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers, but you can integrate monitoring tools with Statuspage, or use our API to programmatically update your page.

capability_claims.claim: Statuspage leverages dependable, redundant systems to make sure audiences get the right message at the right time when a service is down, enabling companies to build customer trust via transparent communication even during downtime.
capability_claims.source_span: Customers. Employees. End users. All of these groups (and more!) want to be notified when your service is down. Statuspage leverages dependable, redundant systems to make sure your audiences get the right message at the right time, every time.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Atlassian Statuspage is hosted on Atlassian's own globally distributed infrastructure, not on Railway's GCP-hosted systems. When GCP suspends Railway's account and takes the API, CloudSQL, and GCP-hosted compute offline simultaneously, Statuspage continues to serve Railway's status page and deliver email and SMS notifications to subscribers via Atlassian's own notification infrastructure. An on-call SRE can update Railway's Statuspage from any internet-connected device (including a personal device not depending on GCP), publishing an initial incident notice within minutes of detecting the outage. This directly satisfies MC-OPS-018's 2-hour customer-communication requirement even when GCP-dependent communication channels are unavailable.
grounding_spans: Statuspage is a communication tool that helps you inform your users about outages and scheduled maintenance.
Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers

enabling_features.feature: Hosted Status Page Independent of Customer Infrastructure
enabling_features.what_it_does: Operates on Atlassian's own infrastructure, not on the customer's servers, ensuring the status page remains accessible to customers even when the customer's entire cloud environment is offline.
enabling_features.source_span: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers, but you can integrate monitoring tools with Statuspage, or use our API to programmatically update your page.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Sends incident notifications to subscribers via email and SMS using Atlassian's own delivery infrastructure, providing a communication channel that does not route through the customer's GCP-hosted systems.
enabling_features.source_span: Your users can subscribe to updates via email or text messages when you have an incident

enabling_features.what_it_does: Supports four incident status stages (Investigating, Identified, Monitoring, Resolved) with subscriber notifications for each update, enabling systematic real-time communication throughout an outage.
enabling_features.source_span: Incidents have 4 different statuses. You can move incidents to these statuses throughout the life of the incident and you can send notifications for each incident update to your subscribers.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Uses Atlassian's own redundant systems to ensure notifications reach customers during downtime, removing dependency on the customer's operational infrastructure for incident communications.
enabling_features.source_span: Statuspage leverages dependable, redundant systems to make sure your audiences get the right message at the right time, every time.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Pre-configure Railway's Statuspage with components mapping to Railway's API, routing plane, databases, and regional compute — so an on-call SRE can publish a targeted incident notice in seconds.
2. Ensure all on-call SREs have Statuspage access via personal devices and non-GCP-dependent authentication (e.g., Atlassian SSO not routing through GCP).
3. Add Statuspage incident creation as step 1 in Railway's GCP account-suspension recovery runbook, with a target of publishing an initial 'Investigating' notice within 15 minutes of outage detection.
4. Configure automated incident-detection triggers from Datadog External Provider Status (or a non-GCP synthetic monitor) to Railway's Statuspage via Atlassian's API, enabling automated publishing independent of GCP-hosted systems.
5. Update the Statuspage incident with 'Identified' status once the GCP suspension root cause is confirmed, and with 'Monitoring' status once the recovery runbook's partial-service restoration track is activated.
6. Publish a final 'Resolved' update with a link to the architectural-change commitment required under MC-REP-005.
grounding_spans: Statuspage is a communication tool that helps you inform your users about outages and scheduled maintenance.
Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers, but you can integrate monitoring tools with Statuspage, or use our API to programmatically update your page.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Status page infrastructure independent of GCP
dimension_coverage.note: Statuspage is hosted on Atlassian's own infrastructure and explicitly does not monitor or depend on the customer's own servers.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers

dimension_coverage.dimension: Automated incident detection trigger independent of GCP-hosted monitoring agents
dimension_coverage.note: Statuspage itself does not do any monitoring — automated incident detection requires integration with a non-GCP monitoring tool (e.g., Datadog External Provider Status). The Statuspage API and webhook ingestion can receive signals from such tools but the customer must configure the monitoring source.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers, but you can integrate monitoring tools with Statuspage, or use our API to programmatically update your page.

mitigation_limits.limit: Atlassian Statuspage does not perform automated incident detection; an on-call SRE or an integrated external monitoring tool must trigger the incident publication. If all Railway communication channels depend on GCP, there is a risk of delay in creating the first Statuspage incident notice.
grounding_spans: Statuspage does not do any direct monitoring of your websites or servers, but you can integrate monitoring tools with Statuspage, or use our API to programmatically update your page.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Statuspage directly addresses the MC-OPS-018/MC-REP-005 communication controls with a verified, infrastructure-independent notification capability purpose-built for exactly this type of platform-wide outage scenario, but evidence rests on product-page claims rather than named customer case studies, and Atlassian's brand recognition introduces a mild reputation-halo factor.

incident_handler_vendors.name: Gremlin
incident_handler_vendors.product_name: Gremlin Chaos Engineering Platform – Disaster Recovery Validation
incident_handler_vendors.what_they_do: Gremlin provides a chaos engineering platform used after incidents to validate post-incident architectural changes by reproducing the failure mode in a controlled environment, confirming that the remediations eliminate the original single point of failure before returning to production confidence.
incident_handler_vendors.handles_scenario: Following a cloud provider account suspension that caused a multi-region platform outage, Gremlin enables the victim organization to validate its post-incident architectural changes by running a controlled Blackhole attack against the cloud provider's endpoints, confirming that the revised routing architecture prevents all-regions blackout before declaring the remediation complete.
incident_handler_vendors.fit_rationale: Gremlin is specifically documented for post-incident disaster recovery plan validation via FireDrills, addressing the scenario where an organization needs to confirm that its remediated architecture actually survives a repeat of the original failure. The platform explicitly names 'Third-party provider outages' as a disaster category requiring DR validation, and its Blackhole attack targeted at cloud provider endpoints reproduces the GCP account suspension failure mode precisely.

capability_claims.claim: Gremlin's dependency failure test uses a network blackhole chaos experiment to drop all network traffic to a designated dependency, validating that the application starts up normally and is able to serve customer traffic without that dependency.
capability_claims.source_span: When you do have a dependency that is out of your control, it's critical to understand how the system reacts when it is unavailable.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: After Railway implements the post-incident architectural remediations (TTL hardening, non-GCP routing seeding, CloudSQL replica), Gremlin is used to validate that these changes actually prevent a repeat of the May 19, 2026 all-regions blackout. A Gremlin Scenario targets all GCP endpoints used by Railway's API and CloudSQL with a Blackhole attack, simulating the account suspension. If the revised routing architecture is sound, Railway Metal and AWS nodes continue to serve traffic via independently seeded routing tables for the duration of the blackhole. The Scenario also adds a GitHub rate-limiting stressor as a secondary stage to replicate the compound failure. Gremlin automatically halts the attack if the Gremlin agent loses connectivity, providing a fail-safe for production-grade testing.
grounding_spans: In the context of disaster recovery, we can use Chaos Engineering to recreate or simulate a black swan event.
Gremlin allows you to quickly and safely halt and roll back any ongoing experiments in case of unexpected circumstances.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Automatically stops the attack if the Gremlin agent loses connection to the Gremlin Control Plane, ensuring a post-incident validation exercise cannot itself cause an unrecoverable production outage.
enabling_features.source_span: Attacks will also immediately halt if the Gremlin agent loses connection to the Gremlin Control Plane.

enabling_features.feature: Blackhole Attack Against Named Provider Endpoints
enabling_features.what_it_does: Drops all traffic to GCP hostnames from Railway Metal and AWS nodes, simulating the account suspension failure mode and validating that independently seeded routing tables serve traffic for the required hold window.
enabling_features.source_span: Gremlin also provides a list of pre-defined third-party services that you can select for easy targeting, such as an AWS EC2 region.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Deploy Gremlin agents on Railway Metal and AWS nodes only (not GCP nodes) immediately after the May 21, 2026 postmortem.
2. Once TTL hardening and non-GCP routing-table seeding are deployed (targeting July 31, 2026 per AC-TEC-017), configure a Gremlin Scenario targeting all GCP API and CloudSQL hostnames.
3. Run the Scenario in a staging environment first, confirming the routing-table lock holds for the full 4-hour TTL window before routing to Metal/AWS.
4. Run the Scenario in production with a limited blast radius, monitoring Railway's customer-facing availability metrics via Datadog and Statuspage.
5. Add a GitHub rate-limiting Blackhole as a second stage in the Scenario, replicating the compound failure observed during the May 19-20, 2026 recovery.
6. Deliver the after-action report to Railway's CTO within 5 business days, confirming whether the decoupled architecture prevents all-regions blackout before the Q3 2026 GCP hot-path removal is declared complete.
grounding_spans: In the context of disaster recovery, we can use Chaos Engineering to recreate or simulate a black swan event.
Scenarios let us accurately simulate real-world disaster conditions by incrementally increasing both the blast radius and magnitude of an attack.
Third-party provider outages (i.e. loss of infrastructure owned by a third-party cloud provider).

dimension_coverage.dimension: Compound GCP + GitHub failure scenario
dimension_coverage.note: Gremlin Scenarios support chaining multiple Blackhole attacks sequentially, including a GitHub endpoint blackhole as a secondary stressor.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Scenarios let us accurately simulate real-world disaster conditions by incrementally increasing both the blast radius and magnitude of an attack.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Architectural remediation implementation
dimension_coverage.note: Gremlin validates whether remediations work but does not implement the TTL hardening, routing-table seeding, or CloudSQL replication changes themselves.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: In the context of disaster recovery, we can use Chaos Engineering to recreate or simulate a black swan event.

mitigation_proof.outcome: Qualtrics used Gremlin to test large-scale DR activities across 40+ teams independently, saving over 500 engineering hours and reducing per-dependency test times from several hours to four minutes.
mitigation_proof.source_span: Using Gremlin, over 40 of their teams independently planned for failover, which saved over 500 engineering hours and reduced their per-dependency test times from several hours to just four minutes.

mitigation_limits.limit: Gremlin does not provide legal advisory, contract renegotiation with GCP, or employment law compliance services; its scope is limited to resilience testing and DR plan validation.
grounding_spans: In the context of disaster recovery, we can use Chaos Engineering to recreate or simulate a black swan event.

incident_handler_vendors.score_rationale: Gremlin's Blackhole network attack capability maps directly to the post-incident validation use case (simulating cloud provider endpoint unavailability), and both verified claims confirm this specific mechanism, but evidence rests on product-page-level documentation rather than named customer case studies, and the role is post-incident validation rather than active incident handling, creating a slight adjacency gap on D1 and D3.

internal.recommended_action_for_them: Convene an architectural review within 72 hours to decouple the GCP-hosted control-plane API from the data-plane routing path.
