control_objectives.statement: Ensure operational quality controls for bulk dairy powder production prevent pathogen contamination events that trigger multi-tier downstream recall cascades.
Ensure supply chain visibility and traceability are sufficient to identify and notify all downstream consignees of a recalled ingredient within a defined, bounded timeframe.
Ensure timely regulatory notification and ongoing cooperation with FDA and FSIS during open-ended ingredient recall investigations meeting FSMA reporting obligations.
Protect brand and cooperative reputation by executing coordinated, accurate public communications throughout a cascading multi-brand recall event.
Maintain stakeholder trust with 300+ member dairy families and downstream manufacturer customers during a recall event that disrupts revenue and market access.
Ensure third-party and downstream customer contractual obligations include ingredient traceability and recall-notification requirements sufficient to limit cascade scope.

master_controls.statement: California Dairies must establish and test a validated environmental monitoring programme for Salmonella at its Visalia bulk powder facilities, with documented corrective-action triggers, before resuming commercial shipments of Low Heat NFDM and Buttermilk Powder recalled on April 20, 2026.

CDI must implement lot-level segregation and hold protocols for all bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder batches produced within the implicated manufacturing window identified in the April 20 recall, ensuring no commingling with post-remediation production.

CDI must document and close the specific environmental or process control gap that permitted potential Salmonella contamination of the recalled Low Heat NFDM lots, with root-cause analysis submitted to FDA as part of the corrective action plan under 21 CFR Part 117.

CDI must maintain a real-time consignee registry mapping every wholesale distributor and manufacturer that received implicated bulk NFDM or Buttermilk Powder, enabling FDA to identify all downstream SKUs without relying on self-reported cascade notifications.

Each downstream manufacturer receiving CDI bulk powder must verify its ingredient-lot tracking system can link CDI's recalled lot codes to finished-product batch records within 24 hours of a recall notification, as the Utz and Ghirardelli cascade demonstrated a multi-week lag.

CDI and downstream co-manufacturers (including the Mama Cozzi's and Great Value frozen pizza producers named in the FSIS April 30 public health alert) must activate recall-coordination protocols that include FSIS and FDA joint notification within 24 hours of a new SKU identification.

CDI must submit, and FDA must receive, a complete recall status report under 21 CFR Part 7 covering all consignees notified, consignee responses received, and outstanding unconfirmed downstream products, updated at least weekly while the recall remains open-ended.

CDI must cooperate with both FDA and USDA FSIS under their joint enforcement authority — established by the April 30 FSIS public health alert — by providing a unified ingredient-distribution dataset usable by both agencies without separate formal requests.

CDI must ensure its FSMA Preventive Controls for Human Food plan (21 CFR Part 117) explicitly covers Salmonella as an environmental pathogen hazard for Low Heat NFDM, the product class at issue in the April 20, 2026 recall, and that the plan was current before the implicated production date.

CDI's crisis communications team must issue and maintain a single authoritative public statement page — distinct from the FDA recall page — that 300+ member cooperative families and downstream manufacturer customers can reference, updated as new SKUs are confirmed.

CDI must brief its cooperative board representing 300+ member dairy families within 72 hours of any material expansion of the recall cascade (such as the FSIS public health alert on April 30 and its May 1 expansion) to ensure member-level transparency on reputational and financial exposure.

CDI must prepare and hold ready a media Q&A document specifically addressing the '40% US dry milk powder market share' narrative appearing in trade coverage, to prevent amplified reputational harm from market-concentration framing during the open-ended recall period.

All CDI bulk-powder supply contracts must be reviewed to confirm they include mandatory recall-notification clauses requiring downstream customers to file FDA voluntary recall notices within 48 hours of receiving CDI's recall notification — a gap evidenced by the multi-week delay before Utz, Ghirardelli, and Jonco filed their own recalls.

CDI must conduct a contractual audit of its wholesale distributor agreements to confirm all intermediaries receiving bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder are obligated to maintain lot-level consignee records accessible to FDA during recall investigations, as required under 21 CFR Part 1 Subpart J (FSMA traceability rule).

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: California Dairies must establish and test a validated
adaptive_controls.statement: California Dairies Inc. must, prior to resuming any commercial shipment of Low Heat Non-Fat Dried Milk Powder or Buttermilk Powder from its Visalia bulk powder facilities, establish a validated environmental monitoring programme specifically targeting Salmonella spp. at all processing zones implicated in the April 20, 2026 recall, with written corrective-action trigger thresholds (e.g., zone-1 positive = immediate production halt, zone-2/3 positive = enhanced sampling protocol) documented and approved by a qualified food safety authority. Evidence of completion shall consist of: (1) a signed validation report confirming the monitoring plan covers all implicated zones, (2) at least one completed monitoring cycle with results on file, and (3) FDA acknowledgement of the programme as part of CDI's corrective action plan submission under 21 CFR Part 117 before any restart shipment date.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control establishes the general obligation; this adaptation specifies Visalia as the named facility, ties the trigger thresholds to zone-level Salmonella environmental monitoring consistent with FDA's environmental pathogen framework, and makes FDA acknowledgement of the plan a prerequisite to restart rather than a concurrent activity.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must implement lot-level segregation and hold protocols
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must immediately place and maintain a documented physical and system-level hold on every bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder lot produced during the manufacturing window identified in the April 20, 2026 recall, ensuring those lots are stored in physically segregated areas bearing quarantine signage and are flagged with 'HOLD – RECALL PENDING' status in CDI's inventory management system, with no transfer or release permitted without written sign-off from CDI's VP of Quality and a corresponding FDA recall coordinator. Lot-level segregation records — including warehouse location maps, inventory system screenshots, and transfer logs — must be compiled into a single dossier and made available to FDA inspectors within 24 hours of request, demonstrating zero commingling with any post-remediation production runs.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control addresses the general lot-segregation requirement; this adaptation adds the specific dual-layer control (physical quarantine plus system flag), names the internal sign-off authority, and sets a 24-hour FDA access standard directly tied to the open-ended nature of the cascade in which commingling risk persists as new downstream SKUs continue to be identified.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must document and close the specific environmental
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must complete and submit to FDA a formal root-cause analysis (RCA) report — compliant with 21 CFR Part 117 Subpart G corrective action requirements — that identifies the specific environmental or process control failure that permitted potential Salmonella contamination of the Low Heat NFDM lots recalled on April 20, 2026, including a causal chain tracing from raw-material receipt or environmental reservoir through the point of contamination to finished-product dispatch. The RCA must include: (1) identification of the specific control gap (e.g., inadequate post-pasteurisation environmental controls, raw material supplier verification lapse, or processing equipment harborage), (2) interim containment actions already taken, (3) long-term preventive measures with implementation dates, and (4) a verification schedule; CDI's VP of Quality is accountable for submission within 45 days of the April 20 recall initiation date, and FDA must formally acknowledge receipt before CDI may apply for reinstatement of normal shipping status.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control sets the general RCA and 21 CFR Part 117 submission obligation; this adaptation names the specific product class (Low Heat NFDM), specifies the four required RCA content elements grounded in the actual contamination scenario, assigns accountability to a named internal role, sets a concrete 45-day deadline from the April 20 anchor date, and conditions shipping reinstatement on FDA acknowledgement.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must maintain a real-time consignee registry mapping
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must build and maintain, within five business days of the April 20, 2026 recall notification date (and update continuously thereafter), a real-time consignee registry in a format directly accessible to FDA recall coordinators, mapping every wholesale distributor and direct manufacturer customer that received any bulk Low Heat NFDM or Buttermilk Powder lot covered by the recall, with each entry including: entity name, facility address, lot codes received, quantities, receipt dates, and current recall-response status (notified / confirmed hold / sub-recall filed / none). The registry must be structured so that FDA can extract all unconfirmed downstream recipients at any point during the open-ended cascade — evidenced by at least weekly registry exports provided to the FDA recall coordinator of record — thereby eliminating reliance on the self-reported cascade notifications that left gaps through at least the May 11, 2026 FDA update window.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for a consignee registry; this adaptation anchors the registry to the specific April 20 recall event, mandates the five-day stand-up deadline, specifies the exact data fields needed to support FDA's open-ended downstream product identification effort, and requires weekly active exports rather than passive availability.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Each downstream manufacturer receiving CDI bulk powder must
adaptive_controls.statement: Each downstream manufacturer confirmed to have received CDI bulk NFDM or Buttermilk Powder covered by the April 20, 2026 recall — including but not limited to Utz Quality Foods, Ghirardelli Chocolate Company, Pork King Good, Jonco Industries, and JCB Flavors — must, within 30 days of this control's issuance, complete and document an end-to-end traceability drill in which a test CDI lot code is entered into their ingredient-tracking system and a complete list of affected finished-product batch records and corresponding retail distribution records is produced within 24 hours. Each manufacturer's Quality Director must sign and submit to CDI a one-page drill attestation including the lot code tested, the time elapsed to produce the finished-product list, the retail channels identified, and any gaps requiring remediation; CDI must aggregate all attestations and report results to FDA as an appendix to its next weekly recall status report, flagging any manufacturer that failed the 24-hour standard.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control identifies the 24-hour traceability linkage requirement; this adaptation names the specific downstream manufacturers evidenced in the reported recall cascade, converts the abstract requirement into a concrete timed drill with named output deliverables, and routes attestations through CDI to FDA, closing the multi-week reporting lag observed between CDI's April 20 recall and subsequent downstream filings.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI and downstream co-manufacturers (including the Mama Cozzi's
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI and each downstream co-manufacturer — including the producers of the Mama Cozzi's (Aldi) and Great Value (Walmart) frozen pizza SKUs named in FSIS recall actions tied to the April 30 public health alert — must activate a joint recall-coordination protocol under which any newly identified finished-product SKU containing CDI's recalled bulk powder triggers: (1) immediate internal hold by the downstream manufacturer, (2) a joint FDA–FSIS notification submitted within 24 hours of SKU identification using a standardised notification template pre-approved by both agencies, and (3) CDI's consignee registry updated to reflect the new SKU within the same 24-hour window. CDI's Head of Regulatory Affairs is accountable for ensuring all named downstream co-manufacturers have executed a protocol participation acknowledgement on file within 10 business days, and for submitting a protocol activation log to FDA as part of each weekly recall status update.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control establishes the joint FDA–FSIS notification obligation; this adaptation names the specific Mama Cozzi's and Great Value frozen pizza producers implicated via the FSIS public health alert, mandates a pre-approved joint notification template to prevent the ad hoc lag observed in the cascade, and assigns CDI as the coordination hub responsible for downstream protocol compliance.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must submit
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must submit a formal recall status report to FDA under 21 CFR Part 7.53 every seven calendar days beginning no later than April 27, 2026 (seven days after the initial voluntary recall), and continue weekly reporting until FDA formally closes the recall; each report must include: (1) a complete current list of consignees notified with dates, (2) a consignee-response matrix indicating confirmed holds, sub-recalls filed, and non-responses, (3) a list of downstream SKUs newly identified since the prior report (with brand, UPC, and retail channel), and (4) a written assessment of outstanding unconfirmed downstream products still under investigation, quantified to the extent possible. CDI's VP of Regulatory Affairs is the named accountable officer; evidence of compliance is the timestamped FDA submission confirmation for each weekly report, which CDI must archive and make available for inspection.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control sets the weekly reporting cadence and 21 CFR Part 7 framing; this adaptation anchors the first report date to the April 20 recall trigger, specifies the four mandatory content elements reflecting the open-ended, multi-wave cascade structure (evidenced by the May 11 update still showing only eight events with more anticipated), and designates a named internal officer rather than leaving accountability diffuse.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must cooperate with both FDA and USDA
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must produce and maintain a single unified ingredient-distribution dataset — formatted to FDA's recall traceability data standards and simultaneously usable by USDA FSIS without reformatting — covering all bulk Low Heat NFDM and Buttermilk Powder lots implicated in the April 20, 2026 recall, and must make this dataset available to both agencies within 48 hours of any agency's written or verbal request, eliminating the need for FSIS to issue separate formal document demands distinct from FDA's requests. The dataset must include lot codes, ship dates, quantities, consignee identities, and product application types (e.g., human food vs. meat-and-poultry ingredient) sufficient to support FSIS's independent jurisdictional review as demonstrated by the April 30 FSIS public health alert; CDI's Chief Compliance Officer must certify dataset completeness and dual-agency usability to both FDA and FSIS in writing within 15 days of this control's issuance.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control addresses cooperative disclosure to both FDA and FSIS; this adaptation operationalises 'unified dataset' as a single file with defined content fields that satisfies both agencies' distinct data needs simultaneously, anchors it to the jurisdictional boundary revealed when FSIS issued its own April 30 public health alert, and imposes the 48-hour response standard and 15-day certification deadline.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must ensure its FSMA Preventive Controls for
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI must retrieve and review its FSMA Preventive Controls for Human Food plan (21 CFR Part 117) as it existed prior to the implicated production period for Low Heat NFDM and Buttermilk Powder to determine whether Salmonella was explicitly identified as an environmental pathogen hazard for this product class; if the hazard analysis did not identify Salmonella as a known or reasonably foreseeable environmental hazard for Low Heat NFDM — given its classification as a non-ready-to-eat ingredient with a thermal kill step in downstream processing — CDI must document this as a plan deficiency, update the hazard analysis with supporting scientific justification, and submit the revised Food Safety Plan to FDA as part of the corrective action package under 21 CFR Part 117.150. CDI's Food Safety Plan manager must produce a written gap assessment comparing the pre-recall plan to the revised plan within 30 days of the April 20 recall date, and the revised plan must be in effect before any production restart.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires that the FSMA Part 117 plan cover Salmonella as an environmental pathogen for this product class; this adaptation confronts the specific technical question raised by Low Heat NFDM's status as a non-RTE ingredient (where Salmonella environmental hazard coverage is a contested but critical point post-recall), requires a documented pre/post gap comparison, and ties plan revision to production restart as a hard gate.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI's crisis communications team must issue and maintain
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI's crisis communications team must, within 48 hours of this control's issuance, publish and maintain a dedicated public recall information page on CDI's own domain (distinct from the FDA major-recalls tracking page that has been updated multiple times through late May 2026) that provides: (1) a plain-language summary of the April 20, 2026 voluntary recall scope covering Low Heat NFDM and Buttermilk Powder, (2) a continuously updated list of all confirmed downstream SKUs linked to the recall, (3) specific guidance for CDI's 300+ member cooperative dairy families on financial and reputational exposure, and (4) a dated 'last updated' timestamp updated within 24 hours of any new downstream SKU confirmation or material recall development. The page URL must be communicated directly to all member families and downstream manufacturer customers via email within 24 hours of page launch, and CDI's Director of Communications is accountable for daily page review during the open-ended recall period.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires a single authoritative CDI-owned public statement page; this adaptation specifies that the page must be distinct from and complementary to the FDA tracking page (which CDI does not control), defines the four required content elements, imposes the 24-hour update cadence tied to the open-ended cascade, and identifies the 300+ member cooperative audience as a named primary constituency.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must brief its cooperative board representing 300+
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI's executive leadership must brief the cooperative board representing CDI's 300+ member dairy families within 72 hours of each material expansion of the recall cascade, including at minimum: the April 30, 2026 FSIS public health alert, its May 1 expansion, and any subsequent addition of five or more new downstream SKUs within a seven-day window; each briefing must be documented in written board communication (memo or recorded meeting minutes) and must cover the additional SKUs confirmed, the estimated financial exposure to CDI and its members, the current status of FDA and FSIS investigations, and CDI's corrective actions underway. CDI's CEO is accountable for ensuring each 72-hour briefing occurs and that a signed acknowledgement of receipt is obtained from the board chair; all briefing records must be retained for a minimum of three years as part of CDI's recall documentation file.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control sets the 72-hour board briefing obligation; this adaptation names the specific trigger events (April 30 FSIS public health alert, May 1 expansion) and adds a forward-looking quantitative trigger (five or more new SKUs in seven days) to capture ongoing cascade growth, specifies the required content elements, and assigns CEO accountability with board-chair acknowledgement as the evidence of completion.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must prepare and hold ready a media
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI's communications and legal teams must, within 10 business days of this control's issuance, jointly prepare and hold ready a media Q&A document specifically addressing the trade-press narrative that CDI supplies approximately 40% of the US dried milk powder market, framing CDI's response around confirmed facts (the April 20 voluntary recall, the specific product classes recalled, the scope of downstream remediation underway, and the no-confirmed-illness status as of late May 2026) without confirming or amplifying the unverified market-share figure; the document must include holding language for scenarios where the market-share claim is pressed by journalists, and must be reviewed and approved by CDI's General Counsel before use. The Q&A must be updated within 24 hours if the market-share figure is formally confirmed or publicly attributed to a verifiable source, and CDI's Director of Communications must confirm the document is briefed to all designated media spokespersons before any press engagement on the recall.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control identifies the 40% market-share narrative as a reputational risk requiring proactive preparation; this adaptation operationalises the document with specific holding-language requirements, anchors it to the confirmed facts (voluntary recall, no confirmed illnesses) that CDI can stand behind, and explicitly addresses the unconfirmed nature of the market-share figure by prohibiting inadvertent amplification while preparing for the scenario where it is confirmed.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: All CDI bulk-powder supply contracts must be reviewed
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI's legal and procurement teams must, within 20 business days of this control's issuance, complete a clause-by-clause review of all active bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder supply contracts with downstream manufacturers — including those covering Utz Quality Foods, Ghirardelli Chocolate Company, Pork King Good, Jonco Industries, and JCB Flavors — to identify whether each contract contains an explicit mandatory recall-notification clause requiring the downstream customer to file a voluntary FDA recall notice within 48 hours of receiving CDI's written recall notification; contracts lacking such a clause must be flagged as deficient, and CDI's General Counsel must prepare and dispatch contract amendment notices to all deficient counterparties within 30 days, with a target of executed amendments before any restart of recalled-product shipments. A contract compliance matrix — listing each counterparty, current clause status (present / absent / ambiguous), and amendment execution date — must be submitted to CDI's board as an attachment to the next scheduled board recall briefing.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control identifies the missing 48-hour downstream recall-notification clause as a contractual gap; this adaptation names the specific counterparties who evidenced the gap through multi-week filing delays following the April 20 recall, sets the 48-hour standard as the contractual floor, and creates a board-visible compliance matrix so the gap is tracked at governance level rather than remaining within procurement.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: CDI must conduct a contractual audit of its
adaptive_controls.statement: CDI's procurement and compliance teams must, within 20 business days of this control's issuance, audit all wholesale distributor agreements under which bulk Low Heat NFDM or Buttermilk Powder covered by the April 20, 2026 recall was distributed, confirming whether each agreement contains an explicit contractual obligation for the distributor to maintain lot-level consignee records — including onward customer identities, quantities, and receipt dates — accessible to FDA within 72 hours of a recall investigation request as required under 21 CFR Part 1 Subpart J (FSMA traceability rule); any distributor agreement lacking this obligation must be identified in a written deficiency register, and CDI must issue a cure notice or contract amendment to each deficient distributor within 15 days of deficiency identification. CDI's Chief Procurement Officer must certify audit completion to FDA as part of the next weekly recall status report, and the deficiency register must be retained as part of CDI's recall documentation file for a minimum of three years.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control sets the 21 CFR Part 1 Subpart J traceability obligation for distributor contracts; this adaptation anchors the audit to the specific April 20 recall event and the wholesale distribution channels through which CDI's ingredient reached the downstream manufacturers identified in the cascade, sets the 72-hour FDA access standard consistent with the open-ended investigation timeline, and routes audit results through the established weekly FDA reporting channel.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: SafetyChain Software
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: SafetyChain Food Safety Programs – Environmental Monitoring and CAPA
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: SafetyChain is a digital plant management platform for food and beverage manufacturers that digitises food safety programmes including Pre-Op hygiene, HACCP monitoring, sanitation checks, and environmental monitoring with built-in CAPA workflows and exception-based oversight.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: SafetyChain's food safety programmes platform digitises and enforces sanitation checks and environmental monitoring with documented corrective-action triggers (CCP deviations, HACCP verification failures) and automatically surfaces out-of-spec results, directly supporting the validated EMP and corrective-action trigger requirements of MC-OPS-005. Its CAPA module supports root-cause analysis documentation required by MC-OPS-025 under 21 CFR Part 117.

capability_claims.claim: SafetyChain standardises and enforces Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure, ensuring problems are resolved, not repeated.
capability_claims.source_span: Standardize and enforce Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure—ensuring problems are resolved, not repeated.

capability_claims.claim: SafetyChain executes HACCP monitoring and required verification workflows with built-in accountability, so missed critical limits drive immediate corrective action, verification, and traceable documentation, supporting defensible HACCP programmes.
capability_claims.source_span: Execute HACCP monitoring and required verification workflows with built-in accountability, so missed critical limits drive immediate corrective action, verification, and traceable documentation—supporting defensible HACCP programs.

capability_claims.claim: SafetyChain CAPA Management helps food and beverage plants resolve issues faster with built-in accountability, clear documentation, and zero paper chasing, with CAPA processes covering deviations, audit findings, and documentation gaps.
capability_claims.source_span: SafetyChain CAPA Management helps food and beverage plants resolve issues faster with built-in accountability, clear documentation, and zero paper chasing.

capability_claims.claim: SafetyChain's CAPA module links corrective actions to pre-op checks, CCP verifications, internal audits, and other records, enabling investigation and response without jumping between systems, and standardised workflows help teams investigate root causes and complete preventive steps so problems don't resurface.
capability_claims.source_span: Standardized workflows help teams investigate root causes and complete preventive steps, so problems don't resurface.

framework_alignments.framework: FSMA Preventive Controls for Human Food (21 CFR Part 117)
framework_alignments.clause: Subpart C – Hazard Analysis and Risk-Based Preventive Controls; corrective actions and verification
framework_alignments.source_span: Under FSMA, Corrective/Preventive Actions (CAPAs) must be in place when preventive controls fail.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: SafetyChain digitises the environmental monitoring programme by replacing paper-based sanitation and microbiological checks with digital workflows that automatically trigger CAPA when a corrective-action threshold is breached. When a Salmonella-positive zone-2 swab result is entered, the platform immediately surfaces the exception, assigns a CAPA task with deadline to the sanitation or QA team, links the CAPA to the originating check record, and requires documented closure before the issue is marked resolved. For MC-OPS-025, the CAPA module enforces a root-cause documentation step before a CAPA can be closed, creating the 21 CFR Part 117 corrective-action record that CDI must submit to FDA.
grounding_spans: Standardize and enforce Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure—ensuring problems are resolved, not repeated.

Execute HACCP monitoring and required verification workflows with built-in accountability, so missed critical limits drive immediate corrective action, verification, and traceable documentation—supporting defensible HACCP programs.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Automatically surfaces out-of-spec results and incomplete records so QA teams focus on what needs attention rather than reviewing all records manually.
enabling_features.source_span: Automatically surface out-of-spec results and incomplete records so teams focus on what needs attention.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Ensures required corrective actions and supervisor reviews happen on time, every time, with automated scheduling and documented closure tied to the originating check.
enabling_features.source_span: Built-In Scheduling, Verification, and CAPA. Ensure required checks, corrective actions, and supervisor reviews happen on time, every time, without manual follow-up.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Standardises Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, and sanitation checks across all lines and shifts with centrally verified corrective action, creating defensible environmental monitoring documentation.
enabling_features.source_span: Facility and Hygiene Programs. Standardize and enforce Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure

enabling_features.what_it_does: Automatically connects supporting records and documents to each CAPA so investigation records are accessible during FDA audits or root-cause investigations.
enabling_features.source_span: Automatically connect supporting records and documents to each CAPA so you're not scrambling during audits or investigations.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Configure EMP zones: Set up digital forms in SafetyChain for each Visalia facility processing zone (Zone 1–4), specifying Salmonella as the target organism and defining corrective-action trigger thresholds (zone-1 positive = immediate production halt; zone-2/3 positive = enhanced sampling protocol).
2. Execute monitoring cycle: Quality technicians complete environmental swab records using SafetyChain's digital forms at the point of work, with results captured in real time and routed to a central dashboard.
3. Auto-trigger CAPA on positive: When a zone-2 or zone-3 result breaches threshold, the platform automatically creates a CAPA task, assigns it to the responsible QA lead, and sets a deadline — no paper or manual escalation required.
4. Investigate root cause: The QA team documents the root-cause investigation within the CAPA record, linking it to the originating environmental monitoring check, sanitation records, and any prior corrective actions, producing the 21 CFR Part 117 corrective-action documentation.
5. Complete verification before restart: The CAPA record enforces a verification step — requiring documented negative re-test results — before the corrective action can be closed and production restart authorised.
6. Export compliance dossier: Generate the signed validation report and completed monitoring cycle records from SafetyChain as a PDF export package for submission to FDA as part of CDI's corrective action plan.
grounding_spans: Standardize and enforce Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure—ensuring problems are resolved, not repeated.
Centralized, Defensible Records. Maintain secure digital records with timestamps, signoffs, corrective actions, and full audit history across all programs.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Validated EMP specifically targeting Salmonella at Visalia processing zones
dimension_coverage.note: SafetyChain provides the digital infrastructure to configure and execute any pathogen-targeted EMP, but the scientific validation of the monitoring plan and the choice of Salmonella as the target organism must be performed by a qualified food safety authority — the platform itself does not provide validation services.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Standardize and enforce Pre-Op inspections, GMPs, sanitation checks, and facility audits so deviations trigger corrective actions, verification steps, and documented closure

dimension_coverage.dimension: Written corrective-action trigger thresholds documented and approvable
dimension_coverage.note: SafetyChain's digital forms enforce configurable corrective-action thresholds and require documented closure, producing the written trigger documentation required by MC-OPS-005.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Execute HACCP monitoring and required verification workflows with built-in accountability, so missed critical limits drive immediate corrective action, verification, and traceable documentation

dimension_coverage.dimension: Completed monitoring cycle records on file for FDA
dimension_coverage.note: SafetyChain maintains centralized, timestamped, audit-ready records of all monitoring cycles, corrective actions, and sign-offs that can be exported for FDA review.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Centralized, Defensible Records. Maintain secure digital records with timestamps, signoffs, corrective actions, and full audit history across all programs.

mitigation_proof.outcome: One multi-site producer reduced weekly food safety record review from 12 hours to 3 by digitizing Pre-Op and PRP verification and focusing reviews on exceptions that required corrective action — an 83% review time reduction.
mitigation_proof.source_span: 83% Review Time Reduction. One multi-site producer reduced weekly food safety record review from 12 hours to 3 by digitizing Pre-Op and PRP verification and focusing reviews on exceptions that required corrective action.

mitigation_limits.limit: SafetyChain does not provide a scientific validation service for the EMP itself — CDI must engage a qualified food safety authority (e.g., a PCQI) to validate the monitoring plan design before using SafetyChain to execute it.
grounding_spans: Execute HACCP monitoring and required verification workflows with built-in accountability, so missed critical limits drive immediate corrective action, verification, and traceable documentation—supporting defensible HACCP programs.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: SafetyChain's verified capabilities map tightly to the claimed MCs (HACCP monitoring, Pre-Op hygiene, CAPA workflows) and the incident's food-safety contamination scenario is precisely the category the platform is built for, but evidence rests on product-page copy and one framework alignment with no named customer case studies, and the platform's strong food-manufacturing brand recognition introduces modest reputation-halo risk.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Neogen Corporation
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Neogen Analytics Environmental Monitoring Program (powered by Corvium)
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Neogen provides food safety testing products and an analytics environmental monitoring programme platform (powered by Corvium) that enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralises environmental testing data, and automates reporting for compliance and corrective-action conformance.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Neogen's analytics EMP platform is purpose-built for food and beverage manufacturers to establish a validated environmental monitoring programme: it centralises environmental testing data across multiple processing sites, automates compliance reporting, and is specifically documented as improving food safety and quality for companies managing pathogen risk — directly matching the MC-OPS-005 requirement for a validated EMP at CDI's Visalia bulk powder facilities.

capability_claims.claim: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralises environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance, and improves food safety and quality for food companies and consumers.
capability_claims.source_span: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance,

capability_claims.claim: Neogen stated that customer needs during the COVID-19 pandemic, as well as demands for greater transparency in food safety and FDA initiatives, point toward increasing expectations requiring food and beverage companies to implement data-driven environmental monitoring programmes.
capability_claims.source_span: COVID-19 has accelerated the food industry's movement toward its goal of automating and centralizing data collection for immediate visibility and response

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Neogen's analytics EMP platform centralises environmental test results from CDI's Visalia processing zones into a single dashboard accessible remotely, eliminating the paper-based or siloed swab result recording that typically delays detection of harborage events. By automating compliance and conformance reporting, the platform produces a continuous record of Salmonella-targeted environmental monitoring that constitutes the 'at least one completed monitoring cycle with results on file' documentation required by MC-OPS-005 before CDI may restart commercial shipments. The platform's multi-site capability means CDI can monitor all implicated zones simultaneously and compare zone-level performance in real time.
grounding_spans: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance, and improves food safety and quality for food companies and consumers.
automating and centralizing data collection for immediate visibility and response

enabling_features.what_it_does: Enables food safety teams to monitor environmental test results across multiple processing plant sites remotely from a single platform, ensuring all implicated Visalia zones are covered simultaneously.
enabling_features.source_span: enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites

enabling_features.what_it_does: Aggregates environmental testing data from all zones into a single, searchable record that supports FDA review and recall investigation.
enabling_features.source_span: centralizes environmental testing data

enabling_features.what_it_does: Automates the generation of EMP compliance reports, reducing manual reporting effort and producing the documented monitoring cycle records required for FDA corrective action submissions.
enabling_features.source_span: automates reporting for compliance and conformance

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Onboard Visalia zones: Configure the Neogen analytics EMP platform with CDI's Visalia bulk powder facility zone map, designating Salmonella as the target organism and establishing sample site locations for all implicated Zone 1–4 areas.
2. Execute monitoring cycle: Environmental swabs are collected by CDI's food safety team and submitted to an accredited laboratory; results are uploaded to the Neogen analytics platform as they are received, centralising the data record.
3. Monitor results in real time: CDI's food safety director monitors all zone-level results remotely through the platform dashboard, immediately identifying any positive or out-of-spec result.
4. Auto-trigger corrective-action protocol: Upon receipt of a Salmonella positive, the platform logs the event and generates a conformance alert, initiating the documented corrective-action workflow (halt production if zone-1, enhanced sampling if zone-2/3).
5. Complete at least one full monitoring cycle: The platform records the full monitoring cycle — sites tested, results, and corrective actions taken — producing the cycle-completion documentation required as evidence of MC-OPS-005 compliance.
6. Generate compliance report: Export the automated compliance report from the platform for inclusion in CDI's corrective action plan submitted to FDA under 21 CFR Part 117.
grounding_spans: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance
automating and centralizing data collection for immediate visibility and response

dimension_coverage.dimension: Validated EMP specifically targeting Salmonella at Visalia processing zones
dimension_coverage.note: Neogen's platform provides the data infrastructure for a validated EMP; the scientific validation of the programme design (which pathogens, which zones, what frequency) must be performed by CDI's PCQI or a qualified food safety consultant, not by the platform alone.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance

dimension_coverage.dimension: Completed monitoring cycle records on file for FDA
dimension_coverage.note: The platform centralises all environmental testing data and automates compliance reporting, producing the records needed to document a completed monitoring cycle for FDA review.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance

dimension_coverage.dimension: FDA acknowledgement of the programme as part of CDI's corrective action plan
dimension_coverage.note: FDA review and acknowledgement of CDI's corrective action plan is a regulatory process outside the scope of the Neogen analytics platform.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: automates reporting for compliance and conformance, and improves food safety and quality for food companies and consumers.

mitigation_limits.limit: Neogen's EMP platform does not perform accredited laboratory testing of environmental samples; CDI must use an accredited third-party laboratory for the Salmonella testing itself.
grounding_spans: The Neogen analytics EMP, powered by Corvium, enables remote monitoring of multiple processing plant sites, centralizes environmental testing data, automates reporting for compliance and conformance

mitigation_limits.limit: The platform does not submit the corrective action plan to FDA or obtain regulatory acknowledgement; CDI's regulatory affairs team must manage the FDA interface.
grounding_spans: automates reporting for compliance and conformance, and improves food safety and quality for food companies and consumers.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Neogen's EMP platform is genuinely adjacent to environmental monitoring that could catch Salmonella contamination events, but the verified evidence is limited to product-page-level claims with no named customer case studies, no certifications, and no framework alignments, and the platform addresses upstream monitoring prevention rather than the downstream recall cascade scenario at issue here.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: ReposiTrak, Inc. (Park City Group)
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: ReposiTrak Recall Management / ReposiTrak Traceability Network
risk_coverage_vendors.headquarters: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: ReposiTrak operates the world's largest food supply chain traceability network, enabling lot-level tracking of ingredients through the supply chain and providing a dedicated recall management solution that identifies the location of any recalled product and facilitates its removal.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: ReposiTrak's Recall Management solution is built on its supply chain traceability infrastructure and provides real-time lot-level visibility to the location and status of recalled product across distributor and retailer consignees — directly satisfying MC-OPS-029's requirement for a real-time consignee registry. Its FSMA 204-compliant Traceability Network enables downstream manufacturers to link CDI lot codes to finished-product batch records, addressing MC-OPS-030, and its consignee data obligations support MC-TPR-021's distributor recordkeeping audit.

capability_claims.claim: ReposiTrak's Recall Management solution provides real visibility to inventory levels and locations from distribution centres to stores, with product automatically tracked at the lot level, allowing rapid trace-back of a product through a simple search, along with recall status indications and notifications.
capability_claims.source_span: Product is automatically tracked at the lot-level, which allows the rapid trace-back of a product through a simple search.

capability_claims.claim: ReposiTrak's Recall Management solution provides real visibility to inventory levels and locations from distribution centres to stores, maintaining virtual inventories and tracking inventories, returns, shrink and more to provide a more accurate view of the location and status of the product being recalled.
capability_claims.source_span: it is capable of maintaining virtual inventories as well as tracking inventories, returns, shrink and more to provide a more accurate view of the location and status of the product that is being recalled.

capability_claims.claim: ReposiTrak's Traceability Network enables food suppliers to share Traceability Lot Codes and Key Data Elements with downstream customers, enabling rapid identification of affected finished products in the event of a recall.
capability_claims.source_span: Traceability enables faster, more efficient recalls. Some major retailers have already made the decision to trace entire categories or all foods they sell.

capability_claims.claim: ReposiTrak operates the world's largest food traceability and regulatory compliance network, with more than 12,000 retail stores, 8,000 suppliers and dozens of distribution centres using the ReposiTrak Traceability Network to exchange traceability data to meet and exceed the FDA's FSMA 204 food traceability law.
capability_claims.source_span: More than 12,000 retail stores, 8,000 suppliers and dozens of distribution centers use the ReposiTrak Traceability Network to exchange food traceability data in order to meet and exceed the FDA's FSMA 204 food traceability law.

framework_alignments.framework: FSMA Section 204(d) Food Traceability Final Rule
framework_alignments.clause: Additional traceability recordkeeping for Critical Tracking Events and Key Data Elements
framework_alignments.source_span: The ReposiTrak Traceability Network® enables the fast, easy exchange of Traceability Lot Codes and other traceability information known as Key Data Elements (KDEs), the term used by the FDA to describe the specific pieces of data used to track the path of products through the supply chain.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: ReposiTrak's Recall Management solution leverages its existing lot-level traceability infrastructure to give CDI a real-time view of where every implicated bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder lot has been received, maintaining virtual inventory status from distribution centre to store. When CDI or FDA needs to identify all unconfirmed downstream consignees, a simple lot-code search surfaces the complete trace-back, enabling CDI to build and update the real-time consignee registry required by MC-OPS-029. For downstream manufacturers (MC-OPS-030), ReposiTrak's Traceability Network links CDI's lot codes to finished-product batch records at those manufacturers' facilities, enabling the 24-hour traceability drill required by AC-OPS-030-RCL-01. The FSMA 204-aligned KDE recordkeeping obligations embedded in the network also satisfy the distributor lot-level consignee record requirements of MC-TPR-021.
grounding_spans: Product is automatically tracked at the lot-level, which allows the rapid trace-back of a product through a simple search.

it is capable of maintaining virtual inventories as well as tracking inventories, returns, shrink and more to provide a more accurate view of the location and status of the product that is being recalled.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Allows a simple lot-code search to instantly surface the location and inventory status of any recalled product across the supply chain, from CDI distribution centres to retail stores.
enabling_features.source_span: Product is automatically tracked at the lot-level, which allows the rapid trace-back of a product through a simple search.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides alerts and status notifications at each stage of the recall process, enabling CDI and FDA to track consignee response status (notified / confirmed hold / recalled / none).
enabling_features.source_span: Recall status indications and notifications

enabling_features.what_it_does: Maintains virtual inventories of recalled product, tracking returns, shrink, and location changes to give CDI a more accurate view of how much product remains at risk at each consignee.
enabling_features.source_span: it is capable of maintaining virtual inventories as well as tracking inventories, returns, shrink and more to provide a more accurate view of the location and status of the product that is being recalled.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Exchanges Traceability Lot Codes and Key Data Elements across the supply chain network, enabling downstream manufacturers to link CDI lot codes to their finished-product batch records within 24 hours.
enabling_features.source_span: The ReposiTrak Traceability Network® enables the fast, easy exchange of Traceability Lot Codes and other traceability information known as Key Data Elements (KDEs)

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Onboard CDI as the traceability source: CDI provides its bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder lot codes and Key Data Elements (KDEs) to the ReposiTrak Traceability Network for all implicated manufacturing window lots.
2. Map consignee receipt records: ReposiTrak collects KDE receiving records from all consignees (wholesale distributors and direct manufacturer customers) that received CDI lots, assembling the complete lot-to-consignee mapping required by MC-OPS-029.
3. Build real-time consignee registry: CDI and FDA recall coordinators access the consignee registry via the ReposiTrak platform, which surfaces all unconfirmed downstream recipients at any point during the open-ended cascade.
4. Execute lot-code trace-back: For any newly identified downstream SKU, a simple search of the recalled CDI lot code surfaces all consignees that received it, enabling same-day identification of the affected manufacturer and retailer.
5. Track recall status: ReposiTrak's recall status indications and notifications update as each consignee confirms hold, files a sub-recall, or fails to respond, allowing CDI to compile the weekly FDA status report under 21 CFR Part 7.
6. Export weekly registry snapshot: Generate a weekly export of the consignee registry showing notified, confirmed, and outstanding consignees, submitted to the FDA recall coordinator as required by AC-OPS-029-RCL-01.
grounding_spans: Product is automatically tracked at the lot-level, which allows the rapid trace-back of a product through a simple search.
Recall status indications and notifications

dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Summary reports

mitigation_proof.outcome: ReposiTrak operates the world's largest food supply chain network of nearly 30,000 retailers, wholesalers and suppliers, having executed more than 8,000 product recall events.
mitigation_proof.source_span: ReposiTrak, operating the world's largest food supply chain network of nearly 30,000 retailers, wholesalers and suppliers

mitigation_limits.limit: ReposiTrak does not provide FDA regulatory submission services; CDI must separately submit the consignee data to FDA recall coordinators formatted to agency requirements.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: ReposiTrak's lot-level traceability and recall management capabilities map precisely to the downstream cascade scenario (MC-OPS-029/030, MC-TPR-021), with four verified product-page claims corroborating coverage, but the absence of named customer case studies, zero verified certifications, and a single framework alignment cap evidence strength and introduce moderate reputation-halo risk given the 'world's largest' positioning.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: SafetyChain Software
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: SafetyChain Food Safety Programs – Material and Incoming Programmes
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: SafetyChain is a digital plant management platform that digitises receiving inspections and material control programmes with automated escalation and CAPA-driven follow-up, enabling documented lot-level hold and segregation management for implicated ingredient batches.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: SafetyChain's material and incoming programmes confirm receiving inspections are performed, automatically escalate material issues, and drive CAPA follow-up — the platform infrastructure needed to implement and document lot-level hold and segregation protocols for all implicated NFDM and Buttermilk Powder batches, as required by MC-OPS-014. However, physical warehouse segregation and inventory system HOLD flags require integration with CDI's existing inventory management system.

capability_claims.claim: SafetyChain confirms receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.
capability_claims.source_span: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: SafetyChain's material and incoming programmes platform captures receiving inspection data at the point of receipt and, when a hold flag is triggered for an implicated lot, automatically escalates the material issue to QA leadership with a CAPA task. The platform's centralized records and audit history provide the lot-level segregation documentation dossier — receiving records, inspection results, hold status — that CDI must make available to FDA inspectors within 24 hours of request under MC-OPS-014.
grounding_spans: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Documents receiving inspections at the point of receipt and automatically escalates material issues, creating a traceable hold record for any implicated lot from the moment of its identification.
enabling_features.source_span: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Flag implicated lots: Use SafetyChain's material programme to create a HOLD status against every bulk NFDM and Buttermilk Powder lot produced during the manufacturing window identified in the April 20 recall.
2. Document escalation: The platform automatically creates and assigns a CAPA task to CDI's VP of Quality, confirming the hold action and requiring VP sign-off before any release.
3. Maintain audit trail: SafetyChain's centralized records capture all hold actions, lot identifiers, receiving dates, and storage locations with timestamps, building the dossier required for FDA inspector access within 24 hours.
4. Verify no commingling: Generate a report of all post-hold receiving inspections to confirm no post-remediation production runs are mixed with quarantined lots.
5. Release authorisation workflow: Require documented VP of Quality and FDA recall coordinator sign-off as a workflow step before any hold can be lifted in the system.
grounding_spans: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Documented physical and system-level hold on all implicated lots
dimension_coverage.note: SafetyChain digitises the receiving inspection and CAPA escalation, but physical warehouse segregation and quarantine signage are operational steps outside the platform.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

mitigation_limits.limit: SafetyChain does not control CDI's warehouse management or ERP system; physical lot segregation and the inventory system HOLD flag must be implemented in those systems separately.
grounding_spans: Confirm receiving inspections are performed and documented at the point of receipt, automatically escalating material issues so corrective actions and follow-up prevent supplier-related risks from impacting production.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: SafetyChain's verified receiving-inspection and CAPA workflow capability maps well to MC-OPS-014 lot-level hold and segregation needs, but evidence rests on a single product-page claim with no named customer references, no certifications, and the platform's relevance to the downstream cascade scenario is adjacent rather than direct—addressing upstream intake controls rather than the multi-SKU downstream traceability and recall coordination that defines this incident's severity.

incident_handler_vendors.name: Sedgwick
incident_handler_vendors.what_they_do: Sedgwick is the world's leading product recall management company, providing end-to-end recall management services for food and drink brands including regulatory compliance, consumer notification, retailer coordination, consignee identification and management, product retrieval, and real-time recall reporting.
incident_handler_vendors.handles_scenario: Sedgwick manages cascading ingredient-based food recalls for food and drink brands, coordinating consumer notifications, retailer withdrawals, and regulatory compliance across a multi-SKU downstream cascade triggered by a bulk ingredient contamination event — precisely the scenario of the California Dairies Salmonella recall.
incident_handler_vendors.fit_rationale: Sedgwick has managed 7,000+ food and drink product recall and incident response programmes for the world's leading food brands — including cascading recalls triggered by foodborne pathogens and bacterial contamination — directly matching the California Dairies Salmonella cascade scenario. Its documented capabilities cover the full range of incident handler needs: consignee identification and management, multi-channel communication, product retrieval, and real-time recall reporting.

capability_claims.claim: For over 30 years, Sedgwick has been the trusted partner for food and drink brands responding to critical safety incidents including foodborne pathogens and bacterial contamination, across prepared foods, fresh produce, dietary supplements and infant formula.
capability_claims.source_span: For over 30 years, Sedgwick has been the trusted partner for food and drink brands responding to critical safety incidents — from foodborne pathogens and bacterial contamination to allergen mislabeling, packaging defects and more.

capability_claims.claim: Sedgwick delivers comprehensive product recall support for the food and drink sector including regulatory compliance, recall communications, public notifications, and retailer coordination, ensuring swift action to safeguard consumers and protect brand integrity.
capability_claims.source_span: From regulatory compliance and recall communications to public notifications and retailer coordination, our expertise ensures swift action to safeguard consumers and protect brand integrity.

capability_claims.claim: Sedgwick is trusted by 8 out of 10 of the world's leading food and drink companies and has executed 7,000+ product recall and incident response programmes in 150+ countries.
capability_claims.source_span: Trusted by 8/10 of the world's leading food and drink companies, we offer unparalleled reach, deep industry expertise and proven recall and incident response strategies.

capability_claims.claim: Sedgwick provides consignee identification and management services using data-driven strategies, real-time reporting and actionable insights to ensure only impacted customers are identified and communicated with during a recall.
capability_claims.source_span: Accurate customer identification, timely notification and effective tracking are essential for successful recall remediation.

capability_claims.claim: Sedgwick's proprietary data management system provides real-time tracking, visibility, and reporting at every stage of the recall lifecycle, with a centralised platform ensuring precise documentation to help manufacturers meet regulatory requirements.
capability_claims.source_span: Our proprietary data management system provides real-time tracking, visibility, and reporting at every stage of the recall lifecycle.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Following CDI's April 20, 2026 voluntary recall, Sedgwick's food and drink recall team deploys its end-to-end recall management capability: it takes over consignee identification and management, using data-driven strategies and real-time reporting to build and update the registry of all downstream SKUs — including the Utz, Ghirardelli, and Jonco cascade products. Sedgwick manages multi-channel outbound communications to impacted retailers (Target, Walmart, Aldi) and consumers via its contact centre, coordinates product retrieval from retail shelves, and uses its proprietary data management system to provide CDI with real-time tracking and the precise recall reporting documentation needed for weekly 21 CFR Part 7 status submissions to FDA. For the non-regulatory food safety advisory elements, Sedgwick also handles precautionary safety advisories and voluntary withdrawals for downstream co-manufacturers that need to respond to the cascade.
grounding_spans: From regulatory compliance and recall communications to public notifications and retailer coordination, our expertise ensures swift action to safeguard consumers and protect brand integrity.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Handles precautionary safety advisories, urgent notifications, voluntary withdrawals, and in-store inspections for downstream co-manufacturers that need to respond to the cascade without triggering a formal recall.
enabling_features.source_span: Sedgwick leverages its extensive recall experience to address a wide range of non-regulatory incidents. From precautionary safety advisories and urgent notifications to voluntary withdrawals and in-store inspections to verify product removal

enabling_features.what_it_does: Identifies all impacted downstream customers and distributors, issues timely notifications, tracks responses, and provides recall remediation instructions — directly addressing the consignee registry and cascade notification gaps identified in the CDI incident.
enabling_features.source_span: Accurate customer identification, timely notification and effective tracking are essential for successful recall remediation.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Manages inbound and outbound contact centre operations across 150 countries and 50 languages, providing localised consumer and retailer communications during the open-ended cascade.
enabling_features.source_span: Effective multi-channel communication is vital to ensuring a smooth and efficient product recall process. Our contact centers integrate inbound and outbound channels seamlessly, providing timely, precise and personalized assistance at every step.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides real-time tracking, visibility, and reporting at every stage of the recall lifecycle, producing the precise documentation needed for FDA weekly recall status reports under 21 CFR Part 7.
enabling_features.source_span: Our proprietary data management system provides real-time tracking, visibility, and reporting at every stage of the recall lifecycle.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Immediate deployment: Within 48 hours of the April 20, 2026 voluntary recall, Sedgwick activates its food and drink recall team to take over consignee identification and cascade management from CDI's internal team.
2. Consignee identification sweep: Using data-driven strategies and its network, Sedgwick identifies all wholesale distributors and direct manufacturers that received implicated CDI lot codes, building the real-time consignee registry required by MC-OPS-029.
3. Multi-channel notification: Sedgwick issues timely, personalised recall notifications to all identified consignees via phone, email, and postal channels, providing instructions for product hold, sub-recall filing, and return.
4. Downstream cascade management: As new SKUs are identified (Utz, Ghirardelli, Mama Cozzi's, Great Value), Sedgwick coordinates with each co-manufacturer's recall team and issues consumer-facing notifications for each affected retail channel.
5. Product retrieval coordination: Sedgwick manages logistics to retrieve recalled product from retail shelves and distribution centres, with real-time inventory tracking documenting the volume removed.
6. Weekly FDA recall status reporting: Sedgwick compiles the weekly recall status report required under 21 CFR Part 7.53, including consignee notification lists, response matrices, newly identified SKUs, and outstanding unconfirmed products, submitting to CDI for filing with FDA.
grounding_spans: For over 30 years, Sedgwick has been the trusted partner for food and drink brands responding to critical safety incidents — from foodborne pathogens and bacterial contamination

dimension_coverage.dimension: Joint FDA–FSIS notification coordination (MC-REG-008, MC-OPS-031)
dimension_coverage.note: Sedgwick coordinates regulatory compliance during recalls but its documented scope is multi-channel consumer and retailer communications; formal dual-agency (FDA and FSIS) notification coordination requires regulatory engagement that may exceed standard recall management scope.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: From regulatory compliance and recall communications to public notifications and retailer coordination

dimension_coverage.dimension: Crisis communications and public statement management (MC-REP-001)
dimension_coverage.note: Sedgwick manages consumer-facing recall communications and contact centre operations but does not operate CDI's owned media page or cooperative member briefing communications.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: In the wake of a product incident, clear and effective communication is crucial.

mitigation_limits.limit: Sedgwick does not provide the validated environmental monitoring programme (MC-OPS-005) or root-cause analysis (MC-OPS-025) required before CDI may resume commercial shipments; those are food safety and regulatory advisory services outside Sedgwick's recall management scope.
grounding_spans: For over 30 years, Sedgwick has been the trusted partner for food and drink brands responding to critical safety incidents — from foodborne pathogens and bacterial contamination

incident_handler_vendors.score_rationale: Sedgwick is a near-perfect functional match — 30+ years in food pathogen recalls, explicit multi-SKU downstream cascade capability, and 7,000+ programmes executed — but the evidence base is product-page claims and scale metrics rather than named customer case studies, preventing a DOCUMENTED rating despite the capability alignment being essentially exact.

We aim to help our clients address these challenges by helping them design scalable processes and deploy technology-enabled services.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Rapid gap assessment: Deloitte assesses CDI's existing lot-level traceability infrastructure against FSMA 204 requirements, identifying the data, process, and system gaps that allowed the multi-week lag in downstream cascade identification.
2. Consignee data standardisation: Deloitte standardises CDI's distributor and customer KDE records into a format directly usable by FDA recall coordinators, producing the unified ingredient-distribution dataset required by MC-REG-008.
3. Technology-enablement: Deploy or configure digital traceability tools to establish physical-to-digital links between CDI's lot records and downstream manufacturers' finished-product batch records.
4. Distributor agreement audit: Advise on the contractual audit of CDI's wholesale distributor agreements to confirm lot-level consignee record obligations under 21 CFR Part 1 Subpart J (MC-TPR-021).
5. PLM recall impact assessment: Use DPLM to track and quantify the direct financial costs of the recall cascade (customer notification, inventory write-off, legal) and model recovery scenarios.
6. Future-state traceability design: Design the end-state traceability programme for CDI that compresses future recall response from weeks to hours, enabling the 24-hour trace-back required by MC-OPS-030.
grounding_spans: Our food traceability services establish physical-to-digital links to assist with compliance with the FDA Food Traceability Rule
