incident.summary: On 2026-05-21, GitHub CISO Alexis Wales publicly confirmed that threat group TeamPCP breached GitHub's internal repositories by exploiting a poisoned Nx Console VS Code extension installed by a GitHub employee, itself a downstream consequence of the TanStack npm supply-chain compromise on 2026-05-11. The attackers used stolen developer credentials to traverse CI/CD pipelines and exfiltrate approximately 3,800 private code repositories; GitHub stated there was no evidence of impact to customer-owned repositories or enterprises. Secondary victims confirmed in the same campaign include Grafana Labs (which refused to pay a ransom for its stolen source code), OpenAI, and Mistral AI.
incident.severity_rationale: Breach of GitHub's own internal repositories by a persistent supply-chain threat actor, with ~3,800 repos exfiltrated and confirmed blast radius spanning OpenAI, Mistral AI, Grafana Labs and hundreds of downstream npm packages, represents a critical, systemic software supply-chain compromise.

secondary_mappings.why: Attack vector was credential theft from developer CI/CD tokens and OIDC secrets extracted from GitHub Actions runner memory.
GitHub was compromised via a poisoned third-party VS Code extension (Nx Console) which was itself infected through the TanStack upstream npm attack.
~3,800 private internal code repositories were exfiltrated, representing sensitive intellectual property and potentially embedded secrets.
Breach of GitHub's own internal repositories materially undermines trust in its security posture as the world's dominant code-hosting platform.
Ransom demands against Grafana Labs and exfiltration of internal source code create operational and extortion risk for affected organisations.

control_objectives.statement: Detect and contain supply-chain-injected credential theft before developer workstation compromise escalates to CI/CD pipeline traversal and repository exfiltration.
Ensure identity and access controls prevent a single stolen developer PAT from authorising traversal of GitHub Actions workflows and mass repository exfiltration.
Continuously assess and gate third-party software artefacts — including VS Code extensions and npm packages — before installation in developer environments with CI/CD access.

Classify and restrict internal repository data such that exfiltration of approximately 3,800 repositories does not expose customer support data or crown-jewel source code without compensating controls.

Maintain a structured crisis-communications capability sufficient to publicly disclose a breach of GitHub-internal repositories within an operationally credible timeline while limiting reputational harm.
Ensure CI/CD pipeline resilience such that a single compromised developer endpoint cannot become the single point of failure for repository integrity across the entire GitHub internal estate.

master_controls.statement: Implement real-time anomaly detection on GitHub Actions OIDC token issuance events so that the TeamPCP pattern of mid-workflow runner hijack (as documented in CVE-2026-45321) triggers an automated workflow suspension before repository cloning begins.

Enforce mandatory code-signing and publisher-identity verification for all VS Code Marketplace extension publishes from the nrwl/nx publisher account, closing the 11–18 minute undetected malicious-publish window exploited on May 18, 2026.

Deploy endpoint detection on developer workstations with rules targeting the specific Nx Console 18.95.0 persistence artifacts (com.user.kitty-monitor.plist, /var/tmp/.gh_update_state) to enable automated quarantine before CI/CD credential exfiltration occurs.

Enforce hardware-backed phishing-resistant MFA on all GitHub organisation owner and Actions runner service accounts so that a stolen PAT scraped via TanStack npm compromise cannot alone authorise mass repository access.

Restrict GitHub Actions workflow permissions using least-privilege OIDC scopes, ensuring no single runner token can clone more than its designated repository set, eliminating the lateral traversal path TeamPCP used to reach approximately 3,800 internal repos.

Establish a continuous secrets-rotation schedule requiring all GitHub PATs and npm OIDC tokens used by developers who installed Nx Console during May 18 12:30–12:48 UTC to be revoked and reissued within 24 hours of the detection date.

Require an allow-list policy for VS Code extension installations in developer environments with GitHub internal repository access, blocking unapproved versions including any future nrwl.angular-console release not explicitly cleared by the security team.

Mandate SLSA Level 3 build provenance verification for all npm packages in the @tanstack namespace before they are permitted in any GitHub internal build pipeline, given that CVE-2026-45321 demonstrated valid-looking SLSA attestation can be forged by the Shai-Hulud worm.

Conduct a post-incident third-party risk re-assessment of all open-source tooling dependencies — specifically npm packages and VS Code extensions — used by developers with write access to GitHub internal repositories, triggered by the May 11–21 2026 TeamPCP campaign window.

Apply data classification labels to GitHub internal repositories distinguishing those containing customer support interaction excerpts (acknowledged by CISO Alexis Wales on May 21) from pure source-code repositories, enabling prioritised breach-notification scoping under GDPR Article 33.

Implement repository-level data-loss-prevention controls that detect and alert on bulk clone operations exceeding a configurable threshold (e.g. 50 repositories per session), which would have flagged the exfiltration of approximately 3,800 internal repos during the TeamPCP intrusion.

Activate the crisis-communications protocol within 24 hours of internal breach confirmation, ensuring GitHub's public statement — as delivered by CISO Alexis Wales on May 21 — is coordinated with Microsoft corporate communications to prevent conflicting disclosures.

Prepare and issue customer-specific breach notifications to any GitHub enterprise customer whose support interaction data may have been present in the exfiltrated internal repositories, as foreshadowed in the May 21 CISO disclosure.

Isolate and quarantine any developer endpoint identified as having installed Nx Console v18.95.0 during the May 18 2026 exposure window before allowing that endpoint to reconnect to GitHub Actions runners or internal repository access.

Run a tabletop exercise simulating the TeamPCP CI/CD traversal scenario — stolen PAT → GitHub Actions OIDC hijack → bulk repository clone — to validate that GitHub's incident-response runbooks correctly trigger automated pipeline suspension within the 72-hour CIRCIA reporting window.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Implement real-time anomaly detection on GitHub Actions OIDC
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub Security Engineering must, within 72 hours of this control's activation, deploy a real-time detection rule on GitHub Actions OIDC token-issuance logs that fires when a single workflow run requests tokens scoped to more than 10 distinct internal repositories within a 60-second window — the pattern consistent with TeamPCP's mid-workflow runner hijack as documented in the May 2026 CI/CD traversal. Upon rule trigger, the Actions platform must automatically suspend the offending workflow run, revoke the issued OIDC token, and page the GitHub SOC on-call before any git clone or repository-contents API call can complete. Completion evidence: a deployed, tested detection rule with a simulation run log demonstrating automated suspension firing within 30 seconds of threshold breach, signed off by the GitHub CISO or their designated deputy.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses runner-hijack anomaly detection broadly; this adaptation pins the threshold to the specific exfiltration velocity — bulk access to ~3,800 internal repositories in a single campaign — and mandates token revocation before clone completion, closing the exact CI/CD lateral-traversal window TeamPCP exploited between May 11 and May 21, 2026.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Enforce mandatory code-signing and publisher-identity verification for all
adaptive_controls.statement: Microsoft's VS Code Marketplace security team must, within 14 days, implement a mandatory pre-publish code-signing gate for the nrwl.angular-console publisher account requiring that every extension package be countersigned by a hardware-backed key held by Nx/Nrwl's verified legal entity and that the signed manifest hash be committed to a transparency log before the extension becomes publicly downloadable — eliminating the 11–18 minute window during which malicious Nx Console v18.95.0 was live on May 18, 2026 without any publisher-identity check. Any publish attempt that does not carry a valid countersignature must be automatically rejected and the nrwl.angular-console publisher account temporarily locked pending manual review by the Marketplace trust-and-safety team. Completion evidence: a gated CI pipeline configuration for the nrwl.angular-console publisher account, a sample rejected publish attempt log, and a transparency-log entry for a test release, reviewed and accepted by the VS Code Marketplace security lead.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses code-signing and publisher verification in the abstract; this adaptation targets the exact publisher account (nrwl.angular-console), quantifies the detection gap (11–18 minutes on May 18, 2026) that enabled the TeamPCP payload to reach GitHub developer workstations, and mandates automatic account lockout rather than passive alerting.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Deploy endpoint detection on developer workstations with rules
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's endpoint-security team must, within 48 hours of this control's activation, push a detection rule to all macOS developer workstations that holds write access to GitHub internal repositories, specifically scanning for the presence of com.user.kitty-monitor.plist in LaunchAgent directories and /var/tmp/.gh_update_state — the two persistence artifacts deposited by the malicious Nx Console v18.95.0 payload. Upon detection, the EDR agent must automatically quarantine the device (revoke network access to GitHub Actions runners and internal repository endpoints), trigger a Vault token rotation for any HashiCorp Vault, AWS, GCP/Docker, or 1Password credential material associated with the device's logged-in user account, and alert the GitHub SOC with a pre-populated incident ticket referencing the May 18 2026 exposure window. Completion evidence: EDR rule deployment receipt for 100% of in-scope macOS endpoints, a test-environment quarantine log showing network isolation within 60 seconds of artifact detection, and credential-rotation confirmation from the Vault and secrets-management teams.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses endpoint detection for the Nx Console persistence artifacts; this adaptation names the two specific indicators of compromise (com.user.kitty-monitor.plist and /var/tmp/.gh_update_state), enumerates all credential stores the payload was confirmed to harvest, and mandates automated credential rotation as an immediate quarantine step rather than a post-analysis action.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Enforce hardware-backed phishing-resistant MFA on all GitHub organisation
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's identity and access management team must, within 24 hours, identify every GitHub organisation owner account and every Actions runner service account whose PAT or OIDC token could have been harvested by the Nx Console v18.95.0 payload (specifically those belonging to developers who installed the extension during the May 18 2026 12:30–12:48 UTC exposure window) and enforce re-enrolment into hardware-backed, phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/passkey) before restoring that account's organisation-owner or runner-registration privileges. Accounts not re-enrolled within 24 hours must be automatically suspended from organisation-owner and Actions-runner roles. Completion evidence: a reconciled list of affected accounts cross-referenced against VS Code Marketplace install telemetry for the exposure window, MFA re-enrolment audit log exported from GitHub's IdP, and zero suspended-account exceptions signed off by the GitHub CISO.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates phishing-resistant MFA for GitHub organisation owners and runner service accounts; this adaptation scopes enforcement to the specific population of accounts whose credentials were confirmed at risk from the Nx Console v18.95.0 payload harvesting HashiCorp Vault, AWS, npm, GitHub, GCP/Docker, and 1Password material, with a hard 24-hour re-enrolment deadline tied to the May 18 exposure window.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Restrict GitHub Actions workflow permissions using least-privilege OIDC scopes
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's platform-security team must, within 7 days, audit and rewrite all GitHub Actions workflow permission configurations across GitHub's internal repository estate so that each OIDC runner token is scoped exclusively to the single repository (or explicitly bounded repository set) required by that workflow — replacing any wildcard contents:read or metadata:read scopes that currently permit cross-repository access. A policy-as-code check must be added to the GitHub Actions runner provisioning pipeline that rejects any workflow requesting an OIDC token scope exceeding its declared repository set at job-queue time, preventing the lateral traversal path TeamPCP used to reach approximately 3,800 internal repositories from a single compromised runner. Completion evidence: a diff of all updated workflow permission blocks, a passing policy-as-code gate log from a representative internal repository set, and a penetration test result confirming a single runner token cannot clone repositories outside its declared scope.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses least-privilege OIDC scoping for Actions runners; this adaptation anchors the requirement in the confirmed exfiltration of approximately 3,800 internal repositories and mandates a policy-as-code enforcement gate at job-queue time — the specific control point at which TeamPCP's lateral traversal could have been mechanically blocked.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Establish a continuous secrets-rotation schedule requiring all GitHub
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's secrets-management team must, within 24 hours of this control's activation, produce a definitive list of developers who installed Nx Console during the May 18 2026 12:30–12:48 UTC VS Code Marketplace exposure window and immediately revoke and reissue all GitHub PATs, npm OIDC tokens, HashiCorp Vault leases, AWS access keys, GCP service-account keys, Docker registry credentials, and 1Password integration tokens associated with those developer identities — covering all eight credential classes confirmed to have been targeted by the malicious payload's exfiltration channels (HTTPS, GitHub API, and DNS tunnelling). Revocation must be completed before any affected developer's endpoint is permitted to reconnect to GitHub internal systems, and a rotation-completion report must be submitted to the GitHub CISO by end of business on the day following this control's activation. Completion evidence: a timestamped revocation log from each secrets system (Vault audit log, GitHub PAT admin export, AWS IAM credential report, GCP audit log, npm token list), a reissuance confirmation for each affected developer, and CISO sign-off on the completion report.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control establishes a secrets-rotation schedule for affected developers; this adaptation enumerates all eight specific credential classes that the Nx Console v18.95.0 payload was confirmed to harvest and ties the rotation trigger to the precise 18-minute VS Code Marketplace exposure window on May 18 2026, ensuring no harvested credential remains valid beyond the 24-hour deadline regardless of which secrets system it originated from.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Require an allow-list policy for VS Code extension
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's developer-platform security team must, within 48 hours, enforce a VS Code extension allow-list policy on all developer endpoints that have write access to GitHub internal repositories, initially blocking all extensions from the nrwl.angular-console publisher account not explicitly cleared at a named version by the GitHub security team, and requiring that any future clearance of a nrwl.angular-console release be preceded by a manual binary-equivalence check of the published VSIX against the corresponding open-source build artifact. The allow-list must be distributed via GitHub's MDM fleet-management system and must generate a SOC alert for any attempt to install a blocked version, retaining install-attempt logs for 90 days to support future forensic reconstruction of the May 18 2026 exposure window. Completion evidence: MDM policy deployment receipt covering 100% of in-scope developer endpoints, a sample SOC alert from a blocked install attempt in the test environment, and a written security-team clearance record for any currently approved nrwl.angular-console version.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses a VS Code extension allow-list policy broadly; this adaptation specifically targets the nrwl.angular-console publisher account that delivered the malicious Nx Console v18.95.0 payload, mandates binary-equivalence verification as the clearance gate, and ties enforcement to MDM distribution across the specific developer population with write access to GitHub internal repositories.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Mandate SLSA Level 3 build provenance verification for
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's supply-chain security team must, within 7 days, configure all internal build pipelines that consume packages from the @tanstack npm namespace to enforce SLSA Level 3 build provenance verification at dependency-resolution time, treating any @tanstack package whose provenance attestation cannot be cryptographically verified against a known-good Sigstore transparency-log entry as an untrusted artifact and blocking the build. Because the TanStack npm compromise of May 11 2026 demonstrated that a legitimate release pipeline can be used to publish 84 malicious artifacts across 42 @tanstack packages within a six-minute window (19:20–19:26 UTC), the verification gate must also check that the attested build timestamp and source-repository commit SHA match the corresponding GitHub Release entry, catching pipeline-injection attacks that produce validly signed but mismatched attestations. Completion evidence: updated dependency-verification configuration files for all in-scope internal build pipelines, a test log showing a package with a mismatched attestation being blocked, and a security-team review sign-off confirming the Sigstore transparency-log anchor is correctly configured.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates SLSA Level 3 verification for @tanstack packages; this adaptation incorporates the specific attack vector — 84 malicious artifacts published across 42 packages in six minutes via TanStack's own legitimate pipeline on May 11 2026 — and adds a build-timestamp and commit-SHA cross-check against GitHub Releases to detect pipeline-injection scenarios where a valid attestation is produced for a tampered artifact.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Conduct a post-incident third-party risk re-assessment of all
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's third-party risk management team must, within 21 days, conduct a structured re-assessment of all npm packages and VS Code extensions used by developers with write access to GitHub internal repositories, using the May 11–21 2026 TeamPCP campaign window as the assessment trigger. The re-assessment must produce, for each dependency: (a) the publisher's identity-verification status on npm and the VS Code Marketplace, (b) the time between the most recent version publish and its first internal use, (c) whether the package is in a namespace — such as @tanstack — confirmed to have been compromised during the campaign, and (d) whether the package carries SLSA provenance attestations. Any dependency scoring high-risk on two or more criteria must be escalated to the GitHub CISO within the 21-day window with a documented remediation plan. Completion evidence: a completed dependency-risk register covering 100% of in-scope npm packages and VS Code extensions, a risk-tiering summary, and CISO acknowledgement of all high-risk escalations.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates a post-incident third-party risk re-assessment of open-source tooling; this adaptation operationalises the assessment with four specific risk criteria derived from the confirmed facts of the TeamPCP campaign (May 11–21 2026), ensuring the re-assessment produces actionable risk ratings rather than a qualitative narrative review.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Apply data classification labels to GitHub internal repositories
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's data-governance team must, within 14 days, apply formal data-classification labels to all approximately 3,800 internal repositories confirmed or suspected to have been exfiltrated during the TeamPCP intrusion, distinguishing at minimum three tiers: (1) repositories containing customer support interaction data or excerpts referencing GitHub enterprise customer environments — as acknowledged by CISO Alexis Wales on May 21 2026 — which must be classified as personal-data-in-scope for GDPR Article 33 notification assessment; (2) repositories containing proprietary source code with no customer personal data; and (3) repositories whose contents remain unclassified pending forensic review. Classification results for tier-1 repositories must be delivered to GitHub's legal and privacy team within the 14-day window to enable breach-notification scoping and to determine whether the 72-hour GDPR Article 33 notification window to EU supervisory authorities has been or will be triggered. Completion evidence: a classification register for all ~3,800 exfiltrated repositories, a tier-1 list delivered to legal and privacy, and a written GDPR Article 33 notification-trigger assessment signed by GitHub's Data Protection Officer.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control addresses data classification of internal repositories for breach-notification scoping; this adaptation anchors the urgency in the CISO's May 21 2026 public acknowledgement that customer support data may be present in the exfiltrated repositories and provides a concrete three-tier classification scheme tied directly to GDPR Article 33 notification obligations.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Implement repository-level data-loss-prevention controls that detect and alert
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's platform-security team must, within 7 days, deploy repository-level DLP controls on all GitHub internal repository endpoints that detect and alert in real time when a single authenticated session performs git clone or repository-contents API calls against more than 50 internal repositories within any rolling 10-minute window — a threshold calibrated to flag anomalous bulk access well below the approximately 3,800 repositories exfiltrated during the TeamPCP intrusion. Upon threshold breach, the control must automatically suspend the offending session token, log the full API call sequence with repository names and timestamps to a tamper-evident audit store, and generate a P1 SOC alert. Completion evidence: deployed DLP rule configuration with documented threshold rationale, a test-environment alert log demonstrating automated session suspension at the 50-repository threshold, and a retrospective analysis confirming the rule would have fired during the TeamPCP exfiltration window.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates bulk-clone detection with a configurable threshold; this adaptation calibrates the threshold to the confirmed exfiltration volume (~3,800 repositories), ties the detection window to a rolling 10-minute interval consistent with automated exfiltration velocity, and mandates a retrospective validation against the known intrusion timeline to confirm the control would have been effective.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Activate the crisis-communications protocol within 24 hours of
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's communications and legal teams must, within 24 hours of any future internal breach-confirmation determination, convene a mandatory cross-functional disclosure call including GitHub CISO, Microsoft Corporate Communications, and GitHub Legal to synchronise all public statements before release — applying the lesson of the May 21 2026 disclosure in which CISO Alexis Wales publicly confirmed the TeamPCP breach and the absence of customer-repository impact, statements that required alignment with Microsoft's broader corporate communications posture given GitHub's status as a Microsoft subsidiary. The call must produce a single approved statement, a Q&A holding line for press and enterprise customer enquiries, and a documented escalation matrix specifying who may authorise deviation from the approved statement. Completion evidence: a formalised crisis-communications SOP incorporating the 24-hour coordination requirement, a record of the SOP's approval by GitHub CISO and Microsoft Corporate Communications VP, and a tabletop test log confirming the coordination call can be convened within 4 hours of a breach-confirmation decision.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control activates a crisis-communications protocol within 24 hours of breach confirmation; this adaptation draws directly on the structural complexity of the May 21 2026 disclosure — a GitHub-branded public statement from CISO Alexis Wales requiring alignment with Microsoft corporate communications — and mandates a specific pre-release coordination call and Q&A holding-line to prevent the conflicting-disclosures risk inherent in a subsidiary-parent corporate structure.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Prepare and issue customer-specific breach notifications to any
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's legal and privacy team must, within 30 days, issue written breach notifications to every GitHub enterprise customer whose support interaction data or environment metadata is confirmed by forensic review to have been present in any of the approximately 3,800 internal repositories exfiltrated during the TeamPCP intrusion — as foreshadowed by CISO Alexis Wales's May 21 2026 statement. Each notification must identify the specific repository or repositories involved, the nature of the customer data exposed, the confirmed exfiltration date range (May 11–21 2026), the three exfiltration channels used by the attackers (HTTPS, GitHub API, DNS tunnelling), and the remediation steps GitHub has taken or is taking. For any customer whose data falls within GDPR jurisdiction, notification must also be provided to the relevant EU supervisory authority if the 72-hour Article 33 window has not already been satisfied. Completion evidence: a completed customer-notification distribution log with timestamps and delivery confirmations, a copy of the approved notification template reviewed by GitHub's Data Protection Officer, and a record of any regulatory authority notifications filed.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates customer-specific breach notifications for enterprises whose support data may have been in the exfiltrated repositories; this adaptation operationalises the notification content requirements using the confirmed facts of the intrusion (exfiltration dates, volume, attack channels) and explicitly addresses the GDPR Article 33 supervisory-authority notification obligation for EU-domiciled customers.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Isolate and quarantine any developer endpoint identified as
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's IT operations and endpoint-security teams must, within 6 hours of this control's activation, produce a definitive list of developer endpoints on which Nx Console v18.95.0 was installed during the May 18 2026 12:30–12:48 UTC VS Code Marketplace exposure window, sourced from VS Code Marketplace install telemetry and endpoint-management records, and immediately revoke those endpoints' network access to GitHub Actions runners and all GitHub internal repository API endpoints. Quarantined endpoints must undergo a forensic triage — checking for the presence of com.user.kitty-monitor.plist and /var/tmp/.gh_update_state — before they may be cleared for reconnection; clearance requires a written sign-off from the GitHub SOC lead confirming no active persistence artifact was found or that remediation is complete. Completion evidence: a quarantine list with device identifiers and quarantine timestamps, forensic triage reports for each quarantined device, and SOC-lead clearance sign-offs for all devices returned to service.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates isolation of endpoints that installed Nx Console v18.95.0 during the exposure window; this adaptation specifies the exact data sources for identifying affected devices (VS Code Marketplace telemetry and MDM records), names the two specific forensic indicators of compromise that must be checked before clearance, and sets a 6-hour quarantine initiation deadline to prevent any still-active persistence from reaching CI/CD infrastructure.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Run a tabletop exercise simulating the TeamPCP CI/CD
adaptive_controls.statement: GitHub's incident-response team must, within 30 days, conduct a structured tabletop exercise simulating the TeamPCP CI/CD traversal kill chain in the following sequence: (1) a developer installs a malicious VS Code extension during an 11–18 minute Marketplace exposure window; (2) the extension harvests a GitHub PAT and HashiCorp Vault credentials; (3) the attacker uses the PAT to hijack a GitHub Actions OIDC token; (4) the runner clones repositories at bulk scale across GitHub internal repository infrastructure. The exercise must validate that GitHub's incident-response runbooks trigger automated pipeline suspension, endpoint quarantine, and credential revocation within timelines that satisfy the 72-hour CIRCIA reporting deadline from the point of confirmed breach detection, using May 21 2026 as the baseline detection date. Completion evidence: a completed tabletop exercise report including a timeline of simulated detection and response actions, a gap analysis identifying any runbook steps that would have exceeded the CIRCIA 72-hour window, and a remediation plan for identified gaps signed off by the GitHub CISO within 14 days of the exercise.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The generic master control mandates a tabletop exercise for the TeamPCP CI/CD traversal scenario; this adaptation sequences the exercise steps to mirror the confirmed attack chain (VS Code extension → credential harvest → OIDC hijack → bulk repository clone), anchors the success criterion to the CIRCIA 72-hour reporting deadline measured from the confirmed May 21 2026 detection date, and requires a post-exercise gap-remediation plan with CISO sign-off.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: CrowdStrike
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: CrowdStrike Falcon® Insight XDR – Automated Remediation
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: CrowdStrike Falcon Insight XDR is a cloud-native endpoint detection and response platform that uses behavioral AI and indicators of attack (IOAs) to detect malicious processes, automatically remove artifacts dropped by malware, kill associated processes, and network-contain compromised developer workstations before CI/CD credential exfiltration can occur.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: CrowdStrike Falcon's documented Automated Remediation capability identifies and automatically removes artifacts dropped by malicious processes based on behavioral IOAs — directly matching MC-CYB-038's requirement to detect Nx Console 18.95.0 persistence artifacts (com.user.kitty-monitor.plist, /var/tmp/.gh_update_state) and enable automated quarantine before CI/CD credential exfiltration occurs. The macOS SHAMOS campaign documentation confirms Falcon detects and prevents plist-based LaunchAgent persistence at the behavioral level. For MC-OPS-009, Falcon's network containment capability isolates compromised developer endpoints from all network activity, including GitHub Actions runner access.

capability_claims.claim: When CrowdStrike detects an attack via behavioral IOA, it automatically removes artifacts dropped by the malicious process, kills associated processes, and reverses registry modifications — all automatically without analyst intervention.
capability_claims.source_span: When CrowdStrike detects an attack, it'll safely remove supporting artifacts once malicious behavior is identified through an Indicator of Attack.

capability_claims.claim: CrowdStrike Falcon's automated remediation removes files dropped by the malicious process, kills associated processes, and details all quarantined files, killed processes, and deleted registry values in the Falcon console timeline.
capability_claims.source_span: It'll also automatically kill associated processes and reverse registry modifications. This all happens automatically and saves the time an analyst would need to manually remove these artifacts.

capability_claims.claim: CrowdStrike Falcon detects and prevents macOS SHAMOS malware that configures a Plist file for persistence and saves it to the User's LaunchDaemons directory, using machine learning and behavioral IOAs at the initial stages of the attack chain.
capability_claims.source_span: the CrowdStrike Falcon® sensor's machine learning capabilities and behavior-based detection capabilities (IOAs) can automatically detect and prevent SHAMOS in the initial stages of the attack chain (i.e.,

capability_claims.claim: CrowdStrike EDR can isolate the endpoint via network containment, taking swift and instantaneous action by isolating potentially compromised hosts from all network activity, and the containment persists even if the cloud connection is severed and during reboots.
capability_claims.source_span: CrowdStrike EDR can isolate the endpoint, which is called "network containment." It allows organizations to take swift and instantaneous action by isolating potentially compromised hosts from all network activity.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Falcon Insight XDR monitors all developer macOS endpoints via a single lightweight sensor. Upon detecting behavioral indicators matching the Nx Console 18.95.0 malware pattern — specifically the creation of com.user.kitty-monitor.plist in the user LaunchAgents directory and /var/tmp/.gh_update_state — Falcon's automated remediation automatically quarantines the persistence artifacts, kills associated processes, and issues a network containment order isolating the endpoint from all network access including GitHub Actions runners. All remediation actions are logged in the Falcon console timeline for CISO review. Separately, Falcon Identity Protection can detect and block use of stolen PATs scraped from the compromised endpoint against GitHub's Entra ID-connected authentication flows.
grounding_spans: When CrowdStrike detects an attack, it'll safely remove supporting artifacts once malicious behavior is identified through an Indicator of Attack.
It'll also automatically kill associated processes and reverse registry modifications.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Automatically removes dropped artifacts, kills processes, and reverses modifications when a behavioral IOA fires, eliminating the need for analyst intervention before exfiltration completes.
enabling_features.source_span: CrowdStrike's Automated Remediation can save time and help protect the organization by automatically removing artifacts dropped by malicious actors.

enabling_features.feature: Behavioral IOA Detection for Persistence Artifacts
enabling_features.what_it_does: Detects malware persistence techniques including LaunchAgent/LaunchDaemon plist creation on macOS developer workstations using kernel-level behavioral analysis, firing before persistence survives reboot.
enabling_features.source_span: IOAs can recognize malicious behavior at further stages in the attack chain, including when the threat actor attempts to employ tactics like data collection, persistence, execution of further binaries, and data exfiltration.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Isolates a compromised developer endpoint from all network activity including GitHub Actions runner connections, preventing credential use even if the PAT has already been scraped.
enabling_features.source_span: CrowdStrike EDR can isolate the endpoint, which is called "network containment." It allows organizations to take swift and instantaneous action by isolating potentially compromised hosts from all network activity.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Deploy Falcon sensor to all developer workstations: Ensure all developers with write access to GitHub internal repositories have the Falcon sensor installed and enrolled in a prevention policy with Automated Remediation enabled.
2. Configure macOS persistence IOA rules: Enable the out-of-box Falcon rules targeting LaunchAgent plist creation in user Library directories, ensuring behavioral detection fires on com.user.kitty-monitor.plist creation even for first-seen files with no prior hash.
3. Automated quarantine on IOA trigger: When the persistence IOA fires, Falcon automatically quarantines the plist file and /var/tmp/.gh_update_state, kills the associated process tree (Nx Console 18.95.0 or its injected payload), and logs all remediation actions in the console timeline.
4. Network containment: Simultaneously issue a network containment order on the affected endpoint, blocking all outbound connections including to GitHub Actions runners, preventing use of any PAT already scraped in memory.
5. Alert and escalate: Falcon pages the GitHub SOC on-call with a high-severity alert containing the full attack process tree, remediation timeline, and endpoint identity for immediate PAT revocation.
6. Verify containment: The SOC analyst reviews the Falcon console remediation timeline, confirms artifact removal, and initiates PAT rotation per MC-CYB-017 before releasing the endpoint from containment.
grounding_spans: When CrowdStrike detects an attack, it'll safely remove supporting artifacts once malicious behavior is identified through an Indicator of Attack.
Here, we can see the details of the remediation actions, such as any files quarantined, processes killed, and registry values deleted.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Automated quarantine before CI/CD credential exfiltration
dimension_coverage.note: Automated Remediation fires synchronously when the IOA is triggered, removing artifacts and killing processes before exfiltration completes.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: It'll also automatically kill associated processes and reverse registry modifications. This all happens automatically and saves the time an analyst would need to manually remove these artifacts.

dimension_coverage.dimension: PAT revocation and secrets rotation (MC-CYB-017)
dimension_coverage.note: CrowdStrike detects and contains the endpoint but does not natively trigger PAT revocation in GitHub. A SOAR integration or manual SOC action is required to revoke the stolen PAT.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: When CrowdStrike detects an attack, it'll safely remove supporting artifacts once malicious behavior is identified through an Indicator of Attack.

mitigation_proof.outcome: CrowdStrike Falcon platform successfully blocked the COOKIE SPIDER SHAMOS macOS malware campaign across over 300 customer environments between June and August 2025, detecting and preventing plist-based persistence at the initial stages of the attack chain.
mitigation_proof.source_span: the CrowdStrike Falcon® platform successfully blocked a sophisticated malware campaign that attempted to compromise over 300 customer environments.

mitigation_limits.limit: CrowdStrike Automated Remediation does not natively revoke GitHub PATs or npm OIDC tokens scraped from memory before the endpoint is contained; PAT revocation requires a separate SOAR integration or manual SOC action.
grounding_spans: When CrowdStrike detects an attack, it'll safely remove supporting artifacts once malicious behavior is identified through an Indicator of Attack.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: CrowdStrike's verified EDR capabilities — behavioral IOA detection, automated remediation, and network containment of compromised endpoints — map directly to the CI/CD credential exfiltration vector in this incident, but evidence rests on product-page claims rather than named customer case studies, and the dominant-market-leader reputation introduces meaningful selection-bias risk against a purely capability-led assessment.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Socket Security, Inc.
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Socket for GitHub
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Socket is a developer-first supply chain security platform that analyzes every npm (and other package manager) dependency added in a GitHub pull request for behavioral indicators of malicious code — including install scripts, obfuscated code, use of privileged shell/network/filesystem APIs, and known malware patterns — and alerts developers in the PR comment before the package enters the repository.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Socket for GitHub directly implements the allow-list and behavioral-gate policy required by MC-TPR-005: it detects when a new extension or package version introduces install scripts, obfuscated code, or privileged API access — the exact signals present in the poisoned Nx Console 18.95.0 package — and blocks or alerts before the package is installed in a developer environment. For MC-TPR-021, Socket's continuous monitoring of the entire dependency graph against behavioral indicators fulfils the post-incident third-party risk re-assessment requirement across all npm packages used by developers with GitHub internal repository write access.

capability_claims.claim: Socket for GitHub watches for changes to package manifest files and whenever a new dependency is added in a pull request, Socket analyzes the package's behavior and leaves a comment if it is a security risk, alerting developers when packages introduce install scripts, obfuscated code, or usage of privileged APIs such as shell, network, filesystem, and environment variables.
capability_claims.source_span: Socket alerts developers when packages change in security-relevant ways, highlighting events such as the introduction of install scripts, obfuscated code, or usage of privileged APIs such as shell, network, filesystem, and environment variables.

capability_claims.claim: Socket can detect packages that contain known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.
capability_claims.source_span: Socket can now protect users from known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.

capability_claims.claim: Socket's automated threat detection flagged malicious releases of npm packages including got-fetch after a maintainer account was compromised, detecting suspicious behavior before widespread installation.
capability_claims.source_span: Socket's automated threat detection also flagged malicious releases of got-fetch, a package with 20K+ weekly downloads, after an npm maintainer's account was compromised.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Socket for GitHub integrates as a GitHub App that monitors every PR modifying package.json, package-lock.json, or yarn.lock files. For each new or updated dependency, Socket performs static behavioral analysis — not just CVE matching — to detect install scripts, obfuscated code, network exfiltration patterns, and other supply-chain attack signals. When a developer at GitHub installs or updates the Nx Console VS Code extension or any npm package, Socket detects whether the new version introduces behavioral indicators consistent with the TeamPCP pattern (preinstall hooks, obfuscated code, environment variable access). The alert is surfaced as a PR comment before the package is committed, and Socket's CLI (ggshield-style) can also intercept bad npm install commands on developer workstations.
grounding_spans: Socket alerts developers when packages change in security-relevant ways, highlighting events such as the introduction of install scripts, obfuscated code, or usage of privileged APIs such as shell, network, filesystem, and environment variables.
Socket can now protect users from known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Analyzes every new or changed npm dependency in a GitHub PR for behavioral signals (install hooks, obfuscated code, privileged API use) and posts a blocking or advisory comment before the package enters the codebase.
enabling_features.source_span: Whenever a new dependency is added in a pull request, Socket analyzes the package's behavior and leaves a comment if it is a security risk.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Specifically detects the introduction of preinstall/postinstall hooks — the exact mechanism used in the TanStack/Nx Console supply chain attack — and flags them for human review before execution.
enabling_features.source_span: Socket alerts developers when packages change in security-relevant ways, highlighting events such as the introduction of install scripts, obfuscated code, or usage of privileged APIs such as shell, network, filesystem, and environment variables.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Detects when a specific package version is confirmed malicious and reports it directly in the GitHub pull request, enabling a block before any developer installs it.
enabling_features.source_span: Socket can now protect users from known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Install Socket GitHub App on the GitHub internal organisation: Configure Socket as a required status check on all repositories used by developers with write access to internal repos, so no PR adding or modifying a dependency can be merged without Socket analysis passing.
2. Configure allow-list policy: Define a policy that blocks any new VS Code extension or npm package version introducing install scripts, obfuscated code, or shell/network API access unless explicitly approved by the security team — implementing the allow-list required by MC-TPR-005.
3. Real-time PR comment alerts: When a developer proposes adding or updating a package (including Nx Console or any @tanstack namespace package), Socket automatically analyzes the new version and posts a PR comment identifying behavioral risk signals before merge.
4. Block on known malware: Configure Socket to hard-block any PR that introduces a package version confirmed as malicious in Socket's threat database, preventing any developer from installing Nx Console 18.95.0 or a future poisoned equivalent.
5. Post-incident re-assessment (MC-TPR-021): Run Socket's dependency scan across all repositories used by GitHub internal developers to surface any packages that underwent behavioral changes during the May 11–21 2026 TeamPCP campaign window.
6. Ongoing monitoring: Socket continuously re-analyzes the dependency graph as new package versions are published to npm, alerting when a previously approved package changes in a security-relevant way.
grounding_spans: Socket watches for changes to "package manifest" files such as package.json, package-lock.json, and yarn.lock.

Socket alerts developers when packages change in security-relevant ways, highlighting events such as the introduction of install scripts, obfuscated code, or usage of privileged APIs such as shell, network, filesystem, and environment variables.
Socket can now protect users from known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.

dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Socket can now protect users from known malware by detecting when a bad package version is installed and reporting it to the developer directly in a GitHub pull request.

mitigation_proof.outcome: Socket's automated threat detection flagged malicious releases of multiple npm packages (including 'is' and 'got-fetch') after maintainer account compromises — the same attack vector used by TeamPCP — detecting them before widespread installation.
mitigation_proof.source_span: Socket's automated threat detection also flagged malicious releases of got-fetch, a package with 20K+ weekly downloads, after an npm maintainer's account was compromised.

mitigation_limits.limit: Socket for GitHub operates on package manifest changes in PRs; it does not intercept out-of-band VS Code Marketplace extension installs performed by a developer outside of a git commit workflow, such as a direct IDE extension update.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Socket's verified PR-level behavioral analysis and known-malware detection capabilities map precisely to the TanStack npm supply-chain compromise vector, with real-world flagging of compromised packages like got-fetch demonstrating direct incident-type relevance, though the absence of certifications/framework alignments and moderate reputation as a known OSS security tool temper the evidence and independence scores slightly.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Chainguard, Inc.
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Chainguard Libraries
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Chainguard Libraries provides a malware-resistant drop-in replacement for public npm (JavaScript), PyPI (Python), and Maven (Java) registries by rebuilding every open-source library from verified source code in a SLSA Level 3-compliant, tamper-proof build environment, attaching signed SBOMs and cryptographic provenance to every package so consumers can verify that the binary exactly matches the source and was not tampered with during build or distribution.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Chainguard Libraries directly addresses MC-TPR-009's requirement for SLSA Level 3 build provenance verification for all npm packages in the @tanstack namespace. The product rebuilds packages (including JavaScript/npm) from source in a SLSA L3-compliant factory, provides cryptographic proof that the binary matches source code bit-for-bit, and distributes signed SBOMs — addressing the specific concern raised in MC-TPR-009 that valid-looking SLSA attestation can be forged by the Shai-Hulud worm when downloaded from the public registry.

capability_claims.claim: Chainguard Libraries eliminates 98%+ of npm supply chain malware risk by building every package from its public, verifiable source code in a SLSA L3-compliant system, preventing build-time and distribution-stage malware injections.
capability_claims.source_span: Chainguard Libraries eliminates 98%+ of this risk by building every package from its public, verifiable source code in a SLSA L3-compliant system, preventing build-time and distribution-stage malware injections.

capability_claims.claim: Every Chainguard Library version comes with full provenance and signed SBOMs, so consumers have indisputable proof that dependencies came from the SLSA L3-compliant Chainguard Factory, not from a vulnerable maintainer account.
capability_claims.source_span: Every version comes built with full provenance and signed SBOMs, so you have indisputable proof that your dependencies came from the SLSA L3-compliant Chainguard Factory, not a machine from a vulnerable maintainer account.

capability_claims.claim: Chainguard Libraries avoids malicious threats from npm such as the Shai-Hulud worm with 17K+ built-from-source JavaScript projects that are immune from supply chain attacks at the distribution stage.
capability_claims.source_span: Avoid malicious threats from npm such as the Shai-Hulud worm, is, chalk, and lottie-player with 17K+ built-from-source projects that are ready for your next web app.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Chainguard Libraries integrates as a drop-in upstream registry source in JFrog Artifactory, Sonatype Nexus, or Cloudsmith, transparently serving rebuilt-from-source npm packages with SLSA L3 provenance and signed SBOMs. When a GitHub Actions pipeline installs @tanstack or any other npm namespace package, it receives the Chainguard-rebuilt binary — which was compiled from the original verified source repository in a hardened build environment — rather than the public registry artifact. Because the binary is rebuilt from source rather than distributed from the maintainer's publishing credentials, a TeamPCP compromise of the nrwl/nx or @tanstack publisher account cannot inject malicious code into what GitHub's internal pipelines receive. Every package ships with Sigstore signatures and a signed SBOM that CI/CD tooling can verify before execution.
grounding_spans: Chainguard Libraries eliminates 98%+ of this risk by building every package from its public, verifiable source code in a SLSA L3-compliant system, preventing build-time and distribution-stage malware injections.

Every version comes built with full provenance and signed SBOMs, so you have indisputable proof that your dependencies came from the SLSA L3-compliant Chainguard Factory, not a machine from a vulnerable maintainer account.
Avoid malicious threats from npm such as the Shai-Hulud worm, is, chalk, and lottie-player with 17K+ built-from-source projects that are ready for your next web app.

enabling_features.source_span: Use libraries from verified source in a SLSA L3-compliant factory to neutralize malware, ensuring the binary always matches the source bit-for-bit.

enabling_features.feature: Signed SBOMs and Sigstore Provenance Attestations
enabling_features.what_it_does: Attaches cryptographic Sigstore signatures, a signed SBOM, and SLSA Level 3 provenance to every package so CI/CD pipelines can verify origin and integrity before execution, closing the forged-attestation attack surface identified in CVE-2026-45321.
enabling_features.source_span: Every Chainguard Library ships with Sigstore signatures (cryptographic proof of authenticity), a signed SBOM (complete inventory of components), and SLSA Level 3 provenance (attestation documenting how and where it was built). These attestations let you verify the origin and integrity of every package, ensuring no tampering occurred between source code and deployment.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Integrates transparently with existing artifact managers so developers and CI/CD pipelines continue using standard npm install commands while receiving SLSA L3-verified packages without workflow changes.
enabling_features.source_span: Chainguard Libraries integrates with common artifact managers such as JFrog Artifactory, Sonatype Nexus Repository, and Cloudsmith. Once configured as an upstream source, developers pull trusted dependencies through your existing tools automatically, with no change to your workflows.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Configure Chainguard Libraries as the upstream npm registry in GitHub's internal Artifactory/Nexus instance: All internal CI/CD pipelines and developer workstations pull npm packages through this proxy, which transparently serves Chainguard-rebuilt artifacts for all packages in the supported namespace.
2. Enrol the @tanstack namespace: Ensure @tanstack and nrwl packages are included in the Chainguard-served namespace so that any future compromise of the publisher account does not affect what internal pipelines receive.
3. Enforce provenance verification in GitHub Actions: Configure all GitHub Actions workflows to verify the Sigstore signature and SLSA L3 provenance attestation of every npm package before execution, rejecting any package whose attestation does not originate from the Chainguard Factory.
4. Apply cooldown policy via Chainguard Repository: Configure a 24–72 hour cooldown on new package version adoption, ensuring that a malicious version published by a compromised account (like the 11–18 minute Nx Console 18.95.0 window) is not immediately available to internal pipelines.
5. Streamline compliance evidence: Use Chainguard's automated SBOM and provenance records to satisfy the SLSA Level 3 verification evidence requirement in MC-TPR-009, providing auditors with cryptographic proof of component integrity.
grounding_spans: Chainguard Libraries eliminates 98%+ of this risk by building every package from its public, verifiable source code in a SLSA L3-compliant system, preventing build-time and distribution-stage malware injections.

Chainguard Libraries integrates with common artifact managers such as JFrog Artifactory, Sonatype Nexus Repository, and Cloudsmith. Once configured as an upstream source, developers pull trusted dependencies through your existing tools automatically, with no change to your workflows.

With the Chainguard Repository, you set the rules for every package we haven't built yet. Configure cooldowns, block packages with known CVEs, or enforce license and end-of-life policies to meet your team's standards.

dimension_coverage.dimension: SLSA Level 3 provenance for @tanstack namespace npm packages
dimension_coverage.note: Chainguard Libraries explicitly covers JavaScript/npm packages and names the Shai-Hulud worm (the same malware family implicated in this incident) as a prevented threat.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Avoid malicious threats from npm such as the Shai-Hulud worm, is, chalk, and lottie-player with 17K+ built-from-source projects that are ready for your next web app.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Verification that SLSA attestation is not forged (CVE-2026-45321 concern)
dimension_coverage.note: Because Chainguard rebuilds from source rather than distributing public-registry artifacts, a forged SLSA attestation on a public registry package is irrelevant — the binary consumed comes from Chainguard's own hardened factory with independently verifiable Sigstore signatures.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Every version comes built with full provenance and signed SBOMs, so you have indisputable proof that your dependencies came from the SLSA L3-compliant Chainguard Factory, not a machine from a vulnerable maintainer account.

mitigation_proof.outcome: Chainguard's rebuilt-from-source approach successfully prevented 98% of 3,025 known malicious Python packages from the Backstabber's Knife Collection from reaching users in independent testing.
mitigation_proof.source_span: In testing against 3,025 known malicious Python packages from the Backstabber's Knife Collection, Chainguard's rebuilt-from-source approach successfully prevented 98% of these compromised packages from reaching users.

mitigation_limits.limit: Chainguard Libraries currently covers 17K+ JavaScript npm packages but does not rebuild every package in the npm registry; packages not yet in the Chainguard namespace fall back to public registry trust unless the customer configures a hard block.
grounding_spans: Chainguard is building additional ecosystems based on customer demand.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: Chainguard's SLSA L3-compliant, built-from-source npm registry is a precise technical match to the TanStack/Nx Console supply chain poisoning vector, with verified product-page claims covering 98%+ malware elimination and signed SBOM provenance, but the absence of named customer case studies, zero verified certifications or framework alignments, and the inherent reputation halo of a well-funded vendor in a high-profile space temper the evidence strength and independence scores.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Retainer activation (within 2 hours of May 21 disclosure): Activate the Mandiant Retainer; on-call incident responders begin GitHub CI/CD pipeline forensics within the two-hour SLA.
2. Attack chain reconstruction: Trace the full TeamPCP attack path — Nx Console 18.95.0 install on developer workstation → com.user.kitty-monitor.plist persistence → PAT scraping → GitHub Actions OIDC hijack → bulk repository clone — building a forensic timeline for CISO review.
3. Blast radius assessment: Identify all repositories cloned, all credentials exposed, and all secondary victims (confirming or extending the known Grafana Labs/OpenAI/Mistral AI secondary blast radius).
4. Compromise assessment: Hunt for additional TeamPCP persistence mechanisms, backdoored CI/CD workflows, or additional exfiltrated data beyond the ~3,800 confirmed repos.
5. Crisis communications coordination: Align GitHub CISO Alexis Wales' public statements with Microsoft corporate communications, ensuring consistent messaging and no conflicting disclosures; develop the customer breach notification playbook for GDPR Article 33 and CIRCIA reporting.
6. Remediation and recovery: Oversee PAT revocation, pipeline hardening, and environment restoration, signing off on the remediation completion evidence required by each adaptive control.

incident_handler_vendors.handles_scenario: Unit 42 has published the definitive threat intelligence brief on TeamPCP's multi-stage supply chain attack methodology — the same threat group responsible for this incident — including specific Cortex XDR XQL hunting queries targeting TeamPCP process execution artifacts, file creation patterns, and network exfiltration behaviour. Unit 42's IR team can be engaged to conduct forensic investigation of the GitHub CI/CD traversal using this adversary-specific intelligence.
incident_handler_vendors.fit_rationale: Unit 42 has published primary threat research specifically on the TeamPCP supply chain attack campaigns, providing the most adversary-specific forensic intelligence available for this incident. Their documented XQL queries targeting TeamPCP file and process artifacts (kamikaze.sh, kube.py, tpcp.tar.gz, pglog, .pg_state) and network C2 patterns directly apply to the GitHub/TanStack incident's attacker.

capability_claims.claim: Unit 42 has published comprehensive threat intelligence on TeamPCP's multi-stage supply chain attack operations, including documented Cortex XDR XQL hunting queries targeting TeamPCP process execution artifacts, file creation patterns, and network C2 behaviour.
capability_claims.source_span: The Unit 42 Incident Response team can also be engaged to help with a compromise or to provide a proactive assessment to lower your risk.

capability_claims.claim: Unit 42 provides Cortex XDR and XSIAM detections via Behavioral Threat Protection, Advanced WildFire, and Cortex Analytics to help protect against the initial access, C2, and lateral movement patterns used in TeamPCP supply chain attacks.
capability_claims.source_span: Cortex XDR and XSIAM provide a multi-layer defense — including Behavioral Threat Protection (BTP), Advanced WildFire and Cortex Analytics — to help protect against the initial access, C2 and potential lateral movement described in this article.

capability_claims.claim: Unit 42 identified that TeamPCP's CI/CD attack injected malicious payloads that stole cloud access tokens, SSH keys, and Kubernetes secrets from CI/CD systems and developer workstations, then self-propagated by backdooring every npm package the victim can publish.
capability_claims.source_span: A malicious npm package published as @bitwarden/cli version 2026.4.0 was identified as part of a broader supply-chain campaign attributed to TeamPCP. The package impersonates the legitimate Bitwarden command-line interface (CLI) password manager.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Unit 42's IR team brings adversary-specific intelligence on TeamPCP TTPs to the GitHub forensic investigation — including the exact file artifacts (kamikaze.sh, kube.py, tpcp.tar.gz, pglog, .pg_state), C2 domains (scan.aquasecurtiy[.]org, checkmarx[.]zone, models.litellm[.]cloud), and XQL hunting queries covering TeamPCP process execution, file creation, and network exfiltration patterns. This intelligence immediately cuts the dwell-time analysis phase compared to a general IR firm. Unit 42 also provides interim guidance on GitHub Actions hardening (npm ci, lockfile enforcement, egress restriction on CI runners) that can be operationalised immediately during the response.

enabling_features.source_span: The Unit 42 Managed Threat Hunting team suggests the following XQL queries. Cortex XDR and XSIAM customers can use these XQL queries to search for signs of exploitation.

enabling_features.feature: TeamPCP Threat Intelligence and IoC Database
enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides a comprehensive IoC database including C2 IP addresses, C2 domains, tunneling URLs, SHA256 hashes of malicious files, and self-signed certificate hashes — enabling immediate IOC-based hunting across the GitHub environment.
enabling_features.source_span: Based on the rapid escalation of TeamPCP's supply chain operations, Palo Alto Networks highly recommends that organizations immediately audit the following within their development and production environments: CI/CD pipelines. GitHub PATs. Cloud provider credentials. Kubernetes service account tokens (SATs). Container-based SSH keys.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides immediate actionable guidance including credential rotation, lockfile enforcement, egress restriction on CI runners, and GitHub Actions workflow audit — directly applicable to the GitHub internal environment.
enabling_features.source_span: Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed: npm tokens, GitHub PATs, AWS/Azure/Google Cloud keys, SSH keys and CI/CD secrets. Audit npm packages you maintain for unauthorized version bumps or new preinstall hooks. Review GitHub for unauthorized repository creation, unexpected workflow files and artifact downloads.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Engage Unit 42 IR (immediate): Activate Unit 42 Incident Response; provide the TeamPCP threat brief and XQL queries to GitHub's security team for immediate deployment in Cortex XDR/XSIAM against the GitHub internal environment.
2. IoC-based threat hunt: Deploy the documented TeamPCP C2 domain blocklist and file artifact indicators (tpcp.tar.gz, kamikaze.sh, pglog) across all CI/CD runners and developer endpoints to identify the full scope of compromise.
3. Credential rotation: Execute Unit 42's interim guidance to rotate all exposed npm tokens, GitHub PATs, SSH keys, and CI/CD secrets within the 24-hour window specified in MC-CYB-017.
4. GitHub Actions workflow audit: Review all GitHub Actions workflow files across internal repositories for unauthorized additions (unexpected workflow files, artifact download steps, format-results artifacts) per Unit 42 interim guidance.
5. Blast radius confirmation: Use the XQL hunting queries to confirm whether TeamPCP's CI/CD traversal reached any customer-adjacent repositories, updating the CISO's public statements accordingly.
6. Post-incident hardening: Implement Unit 42's recommended CI/CD policy changes (npm ci, lockfile enforcement, egress restriction to private registry only) as immediate operational hardening before re-enabling affected workflows.
grounding_spans: The Unit 42 Incident Response team can also be engaged to help with a compromise or to provide a proactive assessment to lower your risk.

dimension_coverage.note: Unit 42 is a technical DFIR practice; crisis communications and regulatory disclosure advisory (MC-REP-001, MC-REP-008) require a separate engagement such as Mandiant's cyber crisis communications service.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: The Unit 42 Incident Response team can also be engaged to help with a compromise or to provide a proactive assessment to lower your risk.

mitigation_proof.outcome: Unit 42 identified and documented the full TeamPCP supply chain campaign covering Aqua Security Trivy, Checkmarx KICS, LiteLLM, and Telnyx — the same threat actor responsible for the GitHub breach — providing the IoC database and XQL queries now applicable to this incident.
mitigation_proof.source_span: Cortex XDR and XSIAM provide a multi-layer defense — including Behavioral Threat Protection (BTP), Advanced WildFire and Cortex Analytics — to help protect against the initial access, C2 and potential lateral movement described in this article.

mitigation_limits.limit: Unit 42's XQL hunting queries are optimised for Cortex XDR/XSIAM customers; GitHub would need to translate these queries to its own SIEM tooling if it is not a Cortex customer.
grounding_spans: The Unit 42 Managed Threat Hunting team suggests the following XQL queries. Cortex XDR and XSIAM customers can use these XQL queries to search for signs of exploitation.

incident_handler_vendors.score_rationale: Unit 42's adversary-specific TeamPCP threat intelligence and XQL hunting queries are a near-exact match to the incident scenario, but evidence anchors are product/blog pages rather than named GitHub or peer customer case studies, and Palo Alto's dominant brand reputation introduces meaningful selection-bias risk that caps the independence score.
