incident.summary: New York City Health + Hospitals (NYCHHC), the largest US public healthcare system, confirmed on 19 May 2026 that an unauthorised actor accessed its network via an unnamed third-party vendor between approximately 25 November 2025 and 11 February 2026, exfiltrating data on at least 1.8 million patients and employees. Stolen data includes medical records, diagnoses, medications, Social Security numbers, government IDs, precise geolocation data, financial account credentials, and — critically — biometric fingerprint and palm-print scans that cannot be reset. The incident was reported to HHS on 24 March 2026 and is described by multiple sources as one of the largest healthcare breaches of 2026; NYCHHC is offering 24 months of credit monitoring through Kroll to all affected individuals.
incident.severity_rationale: 1.8 million individuals affected with irreversible biometric data (fingerprints and palm prints) stolen alongside medical, financial, and geolocation records — one of the largest and most harmful healthcare breaches of 2026.

secondary_mappings.why: Attackers maintained persistent access across NYCHHC systems for ~11 weeks, indicating endpoint and network detection failures.
Intrusion originated through an unnamed third-party vendor that had privileged network access, exposing a vendor-risk governance gap.
NYCHHC may have violated federal and state breach-notification timelines; it has not yet reported to state AG offices per legal watchdogs.
Permanent exposure of biometric data (fingerprints) for nearly 2 million patients creates irreversible long-term reputational and trust harm.
Biometric and precise geolocation data were stored without adequate protective controls proportionate to their sensitivity classification.

control_objectives.statement: Prevent unauthorised exfiltration of sensitive personal, medical, and biometric data held across NYCHHC systems accessible via third-party vendor integrations.

Ensure all data categories held by NYCHHC — including irrevocable biometric identifiers (fingerprints, palm prints) and precise geolocation — are classified and subject to proportionate access controls before vendor accounts are provisioned.

Enforce data minimisation principles so that third-party vendors accessing NYCHHC systems can reach only the minimum dataset required for their contracted function, preventing mass-exfiltration of 1.8 million records.
Detect and contain unauthorised network access with sufficient speed to prevent a 77-day dwell-time window of the type sustained in the NYCHHC November 2025 – February 2026 intrusion.
Establish continuous assurance that third-party vendors with network access to NYCHHC systems maintain security controls commensurate with the sensitivity of the data they can reach.
Ensure that business associate agreements with NYCHHC vendors contractually obligate timely breach notification and prescribe minimum technical controls for privileged access.

Meet all applicable regulatory notification deadlines — including HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (60-day HHS filing) and NY SHIELD Act (NYAG notification) — following a breach affecting New York State residents' PHI and biometric data.
Maintain stakeholder trust among NYCHHC's 1 million+ annual patients, the majority of whom are uninsured or Medicaid-dependent, through transparent and timely breach communication.

master_controls.statement: Apply encryption-at-rest and in-transit controls specifically to the file stores from which NYCHHC data was copied between 25 November 2025 and 11 February 2026, prioritising the biometric (fingerprint and palm-print) and geolocation data fields that cannot be reset or revoked.

Classify NYCHHC-held biometric identifiers (fingerprints, palm prints) as a separate highest-sensitivity data tier under the classification schema, requiring board-level approval for any third-party access provisioning to systems containing these fields.

Implement data-loss-prevention controls on all NYCHHC network egress points capable of detecting bulk file-copy operations of the type sustained for 77 days without alert between November 2025 and February 2026.

Establish a biometric-data incident response protocol acknowledging that the 1.8 million fingerprint and palm-print records stolen from NYCHHC cannot be reset, requiring lifetime fraud-monitoring rather than the standard credential-rotation remediation pathway.

Deploy network-segmentation controls that restrict the unnamed third-party vendor's access credentials to the minimum required network zones, preventing the lateral movement that enabled exfiltration across NYCHHC's multi-hospital, 70-location network for 77 days.

Implement privileged-access management (PAM) controls requiring just-in-time elevation for all third-party vendor accounts with NYCHHC network access, replacing the persistent-access model that allowed the November 2025 intrusion to persist undetected until 2 February 2026.

Deploy continuous anomaly detection on all vendor-originated sessions in NYCHHC's environment, calibrated to flag bulk-copy behaviour of the volume and duration evidenced in this breach (77 days, 1.8 million records) within the first 24 hours of occurrence.

Conduct an emergency re-assessment of all NYCHHC third-party vendors with network-level access, using the unnamed breached vendor's access profile as the risk baseline, and suspend any vendor whose security controls cannot be independently verified within 30 days.

Require all NYCHHC business associates with system access to provide evidence of penetration testing and vulnerability management practices, specifically addressing the initial-access vector exploited in the November 2025 third-party compromise.

Insert contractual breach-notification clauses into all NYCHHC vendor agreements requiring the vendor to notify NYCHHC within 24 hours of detecting any suspicious access, preventing recurrence of the at-least-69-day gap between initial access (25 November 2025) and NYCHHC detection (2 February 2026).

Establish a continuous-monitoring programme for NYCHHC's top-tier vendors with PHI access, using real-time threat intelligence feeds to identify indicators of compromise at vendor network perimeters before lateral movement reaches NYCHHC systems.

Document and retain evidence of NYCHHC's compliance with the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule 60-day HHS filing deadline (filed 24 March 2026) and assess whether a parallel NY SHIELD Act notification to the NYAG was required and, if omitted, remediate immediately.

Prepare a detailed regulatory response file for OCR's likely investigative inquiry into NYCHHC's HIPAA Security Rule §164.308(b) business associate agreement compliance, covering the unnamed vendor's contracted security obligations and the post-breach remediation steps taken.

Develop a targeted communication strategy for NYCHHC's predominantly uninsured and Medicaid-dependent patient population — which may have limited digital literacy and financial resilience — providing plain-language guidance on the permanent implications of biometric theft beyond standard credit-freeze advice.

Establish a data-retention and secure-deletion schedule for all NYCHHC systems containing biometric identifiers, ensuring that fingerprint and palm-print data is not retained beyond clinical necessity, thereby reducing the irrevocable-harm surface for any future breach.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Apply encryption-at-rest and in-transit controls specifically to the
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days, NYCHHC's Chief Information Security Officer must commission a forensic mapping of all file stores confirmed or suspected to have been accessed between 25 November 2025 and 11 February 2026, and must verify that AES-256 encryption at rest and TLS 1.2-or-higher encryption in transit are enforced on every store containing biometric fingerprint and palm-print fields and precise geolocation records — specifically because these fields are irrevocable and cannot be reissued to affected individuals. Evidence of completion requires a signed attestation from the CISO listing each remediated data store by system name, the encryption standard applied, and the date of enforcement, with particular priority given to any system accessible to third-party vendor accounts. Any store lacking compliant encryption must be isolated from all external network access immediately pending remediation.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control addresses encryption broadly; this adaptation forces prioritisation of the specific irrevocable data categories — biometrics and geolocation — that were confirmed stolen from NYCHHC's environment during the precisely bounded 77-day window, and ties the remediation obligation to the forensically identified file stores rather than a generic infrastructure sweep.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Classify NYCHHC-held biometric identifiers (fingerprints
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 14 days, NYCHHC's Data Governance Committee must formally amend its data classification schema to establish biometric identifiers — specifically fingerprint scans and palm-print scans — as a standalone Tier 0 (Irreplaceable Sensitive) category, distinct from all other PHI tiers, reflecting that the 1.8 million biometric records stolen in the November 2025–February 2026 breach cannot be rotated or reissued. Effective immediately upon schema amendment, any request to provision a third-party vendor account with read, copy, or query access to any NYCHHC system containing Tier 0 biometric fields must be reviewed and approved in writing by the NYCHHC President and CEO or their designated board-level delegate before access is granted. The Committee must deliver an inventory of all current third-party accounts that already hold access to systems containing biometric fields within 21 days, suspending any account whose provisioning was not subject to equivalent review pending ratification or revocation.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for a highest-sensitivity tier and board-level approval in general terms; this adaptation anchors the obligation to the specific data types confirmed stolen in this breach and requires an immediate retrospective audit of existing third-party accounts that already bypassed the proposed control — directly addressing the governance gap that enabled the confirmed vendor access.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Implement data-loss-prevention controls on all NYCHHC network egress
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 45 days, NYCHHC's CISO must deploy data-loss-prevention (DLP) rules on all network egress points — including those accessible via third-party vendor credentials — calibrated to generate a Priority 1 alert within one hour whenever a single session or coordinated set of sessions copies or stages file volumes consistent with the exfiltration evidenced in this breach (operationally: sustained bulk-copy operations aggregating more than 10,000 records or 1 GB of PHI-tagged data within any rolling 24-hour window from a single account or vendor IP range). Alert thresholds must explicitly cover biometric data fields and precise geolocation records as confirmed high-value exfiltration targets in the November 2025–February 2026 incident. The CISO must evidence deployment with DLP policy configuration exports, test-run logs demonstrating that a simulated bulk-copy event triggers the alert within the required window, and a named Security Operations Center analyst responsible for triage of each alert type.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires bulk-copy DLP detection generically; this adaptation derives the detection threshold directly from the confirmed facts of this breach — 1.8 million records over 77 days — converting the incident's failure mode into a concrete, testable alerting parameter and closing the specific gap where no alert fired for at least 69 days after initial access.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Establish a biometric-data incident response protocol acknowledging that the 1
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 21 days, NYCHHC's Incident Response and Patient Services teams must jointly publish a Biometric Breach Response Protocol that formally distinguishes the 1.8 million confirmed-stolen fingerprint and palm-print records from credential-based breach remediation pathways: the protocol must state explicitly that biometric rotation is not possible, that standard 24-month credit monitoring through Kroll does not address identity fraud vectors specific to biometric compromise, and that all affected individuals must be offered lifetime biometric fraud monitoring — not time-bounded — through a vendor capable of monitoring biometric identity misuse across law enforcement, border-control, and employment screening databases. The protocol must assign a named executive owner (minimum: VP-level or above), specify the process for upgrading affected individuals from Kroll credit monitoring to the biometric-specific service at no additional cost to the individual, and be reviewed annually or following any subsequent biometric data incident affecting NYCHHC systems.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control establishes the principle of lifetime monitoring for irrevocable biometric data; this adaptation operationalises it specifically for NYCHHC's confirmed 1.8 million-record biometric theft, names the existing Kroll credit-monitoring offer as insufficient for the biometric harm vector, and mandates the institutional protocol and accountability structure that must replace standard credential-rotation remediation.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Deploy network-segmentation controls that restrict the unnamed third-party
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days, NYCHHC's network architecture team must implement and document network segmentation controls that confine all third-party vendor accounts with system access to explicitly scoped network zones containing only the minimum resources necessary for the contracted service function — eliminating the ability of any single vendor credential to traverse NYCHHC's multi-hospital, 70-location network as the unnamed vendor's compromised credentials did between 25 November 2025 and 11 February 2026. Segmentation must be enforced via firewall policy or software-defined networking controls and verified by a qualified penetration tester through east-west movement simulation from a representative vendor-access entry point; the test must demonstrate that a compromised vendor credential cannot access PHI stores outside the vendor's contracted scope. The network architecture team must deliver a segmentation map, firewall policy exports, and the penetration test report to the CISO and Legal/Compliance within 30 days, with remediation of any identified lateral-movement path completed within a further 15 days.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires segmentation for third-party vendor accounts in general; this adaptation specifies that the control must demonstrably prevent the exact lateral-movement pattern confirmed in this breach — traversal across a 70-location multi-hospital network via a single vendor credential — and mandates adversarial validation rather than configuration review alone.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Implement privileged-access management (PAM) controls requiring just-in-time elevation
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days, NYCHHC's Identity and Access Management team must replace all persistent third-party vendor network accounts with just-in-time (JIT) privileged access management (PAM) sessions, under which vendor credentials are issued for a maximum session window of eight hours, require multi-factor authentication at each elevation, and are automatically revoked at session expiry with full session logging retained for a minimum of 12 months. This requirement applies immediately to all vendors with access to systems confirmed to have been in scope during the November 2025–February 2026 breach and must extend to all remaining third-party vendor accounts within 60 days. The IAM team must provide the CISO with a vendor account inventory demonstrating zero persistent third-party credentials remain active in NYCHHC's environment, alongside PAM platform configuration evidence and a named IAM owner responsible for ongoing enforcement.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for JIT PAM for third-party accounts in abstract terms; this adaptation directly targets the confirmed failure mode — persistent vendor credentials that remained active and undetected from 25 November 2025 through 11 February 2026 — and specifies the maximum session window, MFA requirement, and log-retention period needed to detect or prevent a recurrence of that specific access pattern.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Deploy continuous anomaly detection on all vendor-originated sessions
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 21 days, NYCHHC's Security Operations Center must deploy or reconfigure its anomaly detection platform to apply behavioural baselines specifically to all vendor-originated sessions, with detection rules calibrated to flag — within one hour — any vendor session exhibiting: (a) data-read or file-copy volume exceeding the 95th-percentile baseline for that vendor's session type; (b) access to PHI data stores outside the vendor's contracted service scope; or (c) session duration or off-hours activity inconsistent with the vendor's established access pattern. These thresholds must be derived from a retrospective analysis of the confirmed breach window (25 November 2025 to 11 February 2026) to ensure the detection rules would have fired within 24 hours of the breach's commencement rather than the reported 69-day detection gap. The SOC must demonstrate rule efficacy through a tabletop or technical simulation using the breach timeline as the test scenario, with results documented and signed off by the CISO.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires anomaly detection calibrated to volume and duration; this adaptation mandates that the specific detection thresholds be reverse-engineered from the confirmed breach parameters — 1.8 million records, 77 days, a 69-day detection gap — so that the resulting rules are validated against the actual failure scenario rather than generic baselines.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Conduct an emergency re-assessment of all NYCHHC third-party
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 14 days, NYCHHC's Third-Party Risk Management function must initiate an emergency re-assessment of all vendors holding network-level access to any NYCHHC system, using the following minimum-risk baseline derived from the unnamed breached vendor's confirmed access profile: (i) vendor accounts had persistent rather than JIT access; (ii) no network segmentation prevented traversal across NYCHHC's multi-location environment; (iii) no DLP or anomaly detection alert fired during at least 69 days of active exfiltration. Any vendor whose current access profile matches two or more of these characteristics must be escalated to the CISO and General Counsel within seven days of assessment. Vendors unable to provide independent verification of compensating controls within 30 calendar days of notification must have their network access suspended pending remediation. The TPRM lead must deliver a completed assessment register — including access-profile findings, escalation decisions, and suspension actions — to the CISO and the Board Risk Committee within 45 days.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for emergency re-assessment using a risk baseline; this adaptation operationalises the baseline by deriving it from the three confirmed technical failure modes of the NYCHHC breach itself — persistent access, absent segmentation, absent alerting — giving assessors concrete, incident-specific criteria rather than a generic vendor risk rubric.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Require all NYCHHC business associates with system access
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days, NYCHHC's Third-Party Risk Management and Legal teams must issue formal written requests to all NYCHHC business associates with system access, requiring each to produce within 60 days: (a) a penetration test report conducted within the prior 12 months by a CREST- or PTES-qualified third party, covering the external attack surface and any access pathways into the associate's environment that could reach NYCHHC-connected systems; (b) evidence of a vulnerability management programme with documented patch SLAs; and (c) a written attestation identifying whether the initial-access vector exploited in the November 2025 third-party compromise — reported to involve a security breach at a vendor whose name has not been disclosed — exists in the associate's environment, and if so, the remediation status and timeline. Associates that fail to provide all three items within 60 days or whose submissions reveal unmitigated critical vulnerabilities in NYCHHC-connected pathways must have their access suspended and be escalated to the CISO and General Counsel. Results must be logged in the NYCHHC third-party risk register and reported to the Board Risk Committee at its next scheduled meeting.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires business associates to evidence penetration testing and vulnerability management; this adaptation ties the evidence request to the specific initial-access vector confirmed to have been exploited at an NYCHHC vendor in November 2025, requiring each associate to self-attest on whether that vector exists in their environment rather than submitting generic assurance documentation.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Insert contractual breach-notification clauses into all NYCHHC vendor
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 30 days, NYCHHC's Legal and Third-Party Risk Management teams must review and amend all active vendor and business associate agreements that include network access rights, inserting a mandatory breach-notification clause requiring the vendor to notify NYCHHC's designated security contact — with a named escalation path to the CISO — within 24 hours of detecting any suspicious or unauthorised access to systems that connect to or process NYCHHC data. The clause must specify that a vendor's failure to notify within 24 hours constitutes a material contract breach, entitling NYCHHC to immediate suspension of the vendor's access and recovery of breach-response costs. This requirement directly addresses the confirmed gap in which NYCHHC did not detect the intrusion through its unnamed vendor until approximately 2 February 2026, at least 69 days after reported initial access on 25 November 2025. Fully executed amended agreements or documented vendor acknowledgements must be filed in the NYCHHC vendor contract register within 60 days, with the status of all amendments reported to the General Counsel and CISO.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control requires contractual 24-hour notification clauses; this adaptation anchors the obligation to the confirmed 69-day detection gap in this specific breach and specifies that late notification constitutes a material contract breach with defined consequences, converting the clause from a notification aspiration into an enforceable remedy tied to the precise failure this incident exposed.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Establish a continuous-monitoring programme for NYCHHC's top-tier vendors
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 45 days, NYCHHC's Third-Party Risk Management function must establish a continuous-monitoring programme covering all Tier 1 vendors — defined as any vendor with direct network access to NYCHHC systems containing PHI, biometric data, or geolocation records — incorporating real-time threat intelligence feeds (minimum: a commercial dark-web and IOC feed updated at least every four hours) configured to surface indicators of compromise at each Tier 1 vendor's external-facing infrastructure. Monitoring must be able to detect credential exposure, ransomware staging activity, and anomalous outbound traffic at vendor network perimeters before lateral movement into NYCHHC systems occurs; the programme design must document how each detection signal would be acted on before reaching NYCHHC's environment. The unnamed vendor involved in the November 2025–February 2026 breach — once identified or confirmed — must be onboarded to this programme retroactively or replaced if access is continued. The TPRM lead must present programme design, vendor coverage scope, and feed SLAs to the CISO and Board Risk Committee within 60 days.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for continuous monitoring with threat intelligence for top-tier PHI vendors; this adaptation operationalises the detection timing requirement — signals at the vendor perimeter before lateral movement reaches NYCHHC — and applies the programme scope directly to the class of vendor whose compromise initiated the 77-day breach, including a specific provision for the unnamed vendor in this incident.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Document and retain evidence of NYCHHC's compliance with
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 14 days, NYCHHC's General Counsel and Chief Compliance Officer must complete and retain a documented compliance evidence file establishing: (a) that the HHS breach notification filed on 24 March 2026 was within the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule's 60-day window from discovery (with the discovery date and filing date recorded); and (b) a written legal assessment — signed by qualified counsel — of whether NYCHHC's obligation to notify the New York Attorney General under the NY SHIELD Act (N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa) was triggered by the confirmed theft of New York residents' biometric identifiers, Social Security numbers, and financial account credentials, and whether that notification was timely filed. If the SHIELD Act notification was required and has not been made, NYCHHC must file with the NYAG within five business days of the assessment's completion. If the held claim that no NYAG notification has occurred is confirmed through legal review, remediation must be completed and documented before any further public statement on breach response is made. All evidence must be stored in a dedicated regulatory response file accessible to the CISO, General Counsel, and Board Audit Committee.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control addresses HIPAA 60-day filing and NY SHIELD Act notification in general terms; this adaptation uses the confirmed HHS filing date (24 March 2026) as the compliance anchor, requires a specific legal assessment of SHIELD Act applicability to the confirmed data types stolen, and incorporates the held claim about potential NYAG notification failure as a mandatory contingency to investigate and remediate — without relying on it as established fact.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Prepare a detailed regulatory response file for OCR's
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 21 days, NYCHHC's General Counsel and Chief Compliance Officer must assemble a dedicated OCR Investigative Response File covering NYCHHC's compliance with HIPAA Security Rule § 164.308(b) (business associate agreements) in relation to the unnamed vendor whose compromise initiated the November 2025–February 2026 breach. The file must include: (a) a copy of the executed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the vendor in force during the breach period, with a legal assessment of whether it satisfied HIPAA's required security obligation provisions; (b) a documented account of NYCHHC's pre-breach vendor security oversight activities, including any risk assessments or security reviews conducted on the vendor within the 24 months prior to November 2025; (c) a timeline of NYCHHC's post-discovery actions from 2 February 2026 through public disclosure on 19 May 2026; and (d) a written summary of all technical, administrative, and contractual remediation steps taken since detection. The file must be reviewed by external HIPAA counsel, marked as litigation-sensitive, and maintained in a form suitable for immediate production to OCR upon request. The General Counsel must confirm file completion to the Board Audit Committee within 30 days.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for an OCR response file on BAA compliance and vendor security obligations; this adaptation specifies the four discrete evidentiary components that OCR investigations typically require for a third-party-initiated breach, anchors each component to the confirmed facts of this incident (breach window, detection date, disclosure date), and requires external HIPAA counsel review to ensure the file is legally defensible.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Develop a targeted communication strategy for NYCHHC's predominantly
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 21 days, NYCHHC's Patient Services and Communications teams must develop and distribute — via postal mail, multilingual telephone hotline, and in-person guidance at all NYCHHC clinic sites — a plain-language Biometric Theft Notice tailored to NYCHHC's predominantly uninsured and Medicaid-dependent patient population, written at no higher than a sixth-grade reading level and available in the top five languages spoken by NYCHHC patients. The Notice must explain, without technical jargon: (a) that their fingerprint and palm-print scans were stolen and cannot be changed, unlike a password or credit card number; (b) what specific harms biometric theft can enable (e.g. fraudulent identity verification, employment screening fraud, law-enforcement database misuse); (c) concrete steps beyond credit freezes — including how to enrol in the offered Kroll monitoring, whom to contact if they believe their biometric identity has been misused, and what NYCHHC will provide at no cost; and (d) a dedicated NYCHHC breach-response contact (phone number and walk-in option) for patients without reliable internet access. Distribution evidence — print run confirmation, hotline call-volume logs, and site-delivery confirmations — must be provided to the CISO and Patient Advocate within 30 days.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control calls for a targeted communication strategy for a vulnerable population; this adaptation specifies the communication channels, reading level, language requirements, and substantive content gaps that exist between the standard Kroll credit-monitoring offer and the permanent, irrevocable nature of biometric theft — directly addressing the confirmed stolen data types and the population characteristics of NYCHHC's patient base that standard breach-notification templates do not account for.

adaptive_controls.parent_mc_title: Establish a data-retention and secure-deletion schedule for all
adaptive_controls.statement: Within 45 days, NYCHHC's Chief Privacy Officer and CISO must jointly commission an inventory of every NYCHHC system and data store that holds biometric fingerprint or palm-print records, documenting for each: the clinical or operational purpose justifying retention, the date of last active clinical use of each record, the current retention period applied, and whether that period is the minimum necessary to fulfil the documented purpose. Within 90 days, NYCHHC must implement a secure-deletion schedule — using NIST 800-88-compliant purge methods — that automatically removes biometric records whose retention period has expired and prevents indefinite retention of biometric data beyond confirmed clinical necessity. The inventory and deletion schedule must be approved by the NYCHHC Privacy Officer and documented as a formal data governance artefact, reviewed annually. This control directly reduces the volume of irrevocable biometric data exposed in any future breach of NYCHHC systems — the 1.8 million fingerprint and palm-print records confirmed stolen in this incident representing the harm surface that an effective deletion schedule would have reduced.
adaptive_controls.rationale: The master control addresses retention and deletion scheduling for biometric data in general terms; this adaptation requires NYCHHC to produce a system-specific inventory anchored to the confirmed data types stolen in this breach and to apply a deletion schedule that explicitly minimises the irrevocable-harm surface — operationalising the control as a direct remediation of the scale of biometric exposure confirmed in this incident.

Its documented capability to scan unstructured biometric data 95% faster with Hyperscan technology and to identify data with legal holds supports the data-retention and secure-deletion workflow required by MC-DAT-025.

capability_claims.claim: BigID leverages pre-built sensitivity classification and security policies that align with HIPAA, NIST, and CISA, enabling effective management and protection of sensitive data including biometric data, with automated remediation actions when biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed.
capability_claims.source_span: Leverage pre-built sensitivity classification, security, and business policies that align with compliance and frameworks such as NIST, CISA, HIPAA, and PCI, enabling effective management and protection of sensitive data.

capability_claims.claim: BigID takes an ML-based approach to automatically classify and tag all sensitive, regulated, and high-risk data — by regulation, document type, policy, attributes, person, and more — covering PHI including biometric identifiers as part of its healthcare data security practice.

framework_alignments.source_span: Leverage pre-built sensitivity classification, security, and business policies that align with compliance and frameworks such as NIST, CISA, HIPAA, and PCI

mitigation_mechanism.summary: BigID discovers all instances of biometric fingerprint and palm-print data across NYCHHC's structured and unstructured data stores using its Hyperscan engine and ML-based classifier, then classifies them as a distinct highest-sensitivity tier. Once classified, BigID enforces per-field access policies that require elevated authorisation before any third-party account is provisioned with read, copy, or query access to systems containing those fields. The platform continuously monitors access to biometric data stores and flags overly permissive permissions for immediate remediation, and enables automated deletion workflows for records beyond their retention period.
grounding_spans: Effortlessly classify and categorize challenging and elusive unstructured data, including biometric data.
Detect and investigate unauthorized access to sensitive, biometric data and pinpoint data sources containing biometric data with overly permissive access.

enabling_features.feature: Hyperscan Technology for Biometric Data Discovery
enabling_features.what_it_does: Scans unstructured biometric data stores 95% faster than traditional approaches using BigID's proprietary Hyperscan engine, enabling rapid inventory of all biometric fields across NYCHHC's multi-hospital systems.
enabling_features.source_span: Scan unstructured data, including biometric data, 95% faster with BigID's Hyperscan technology.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Combines pattern-matching with machine learning and natural language processing to classify biometric data with higher accuracy than regex-only approaches, and supports custom classifiers for unique biometric data types.
enabling_features.source_span: Combine traditional pattern-matching techniques with advanced, ML and NLP-based classification to achieve unparalleled accuracy and scalability in data classification. Customize and fine-tune classifiers to find unique types of biometric data that you may be storing.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Identifies and remediates overly permissive access to biometric data stores, and natively revokes user and group permissions directly from the platform to enforce least-privilege access.
enabling_features.source_span: Simplify the access rights management by natively revoking user and group permissions. Strengthen your Zero Trust strategy and get to a least-privileged model.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Triggers automated remediation workflows whenever biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed outside policy — including pre-built HIPAA and NIST-aligned sensitivity policies.
enabling_features.source_span: Tailor policies that surface alerts and automatically trigger remediation actions across the right people and tools whenever biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Generates data risk assessment reports by data type, location, and residency, providing NYCHHC leadership and board with clear visibility into biometric data risk exposure.
enabling_features.source_span: Automatically piece together a data risk assessment and communicate easy-to-understand insights to your stakeholders.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Connect and scan: Connect BigID to all NYCHHC data stores — EHR systems, imaging archives, HR databases, cloud IaaS, and SaaS platforms — and run Hyperscan to discover all instances of fingerprint and palm-print biometric fields across structured and unstructured data.
2. Classify biometric fields as Tier 0: Configure a custom BigID classifier for NYCHHC biometric identifiers (fingerprint and palm-print scans), establishing them as the highest-sensitivity tier distinct from all other PHI categories.
3. Build biometric data inventory: Generate a complete dynamic inventory of all data stores containing biometric fields, tagged with location, access controls, and current permission status — this is the foundation for MC-DAT-015's board-level approval requirement.
4. Remediate overly permissive access: Identify all third-party vendor accounts and internal users with access to biometric data stores, flag those with overly broad permissions, and natively revoke access where not justified by contracted service function.
5. Enforce provisioning controls: Configure BigID policy to alert and trigger a review workflow whenever a new third-party account requests access to systems containing Tier 0 biometric fields, ensuring board-level approval is obtained before access is granted.
6. Enforce retention schedule: Configure data-aging workflows to flag biometric records exceeding the clinical retention period, marking them for secure deletion and preventing indefinite accumulation of irrevocable identifiers.
grounding_spans: Scan unstructured data, including biometric data, 95% faster with BigID's Hyperscan technology. Save time and avoid sensitive data blind spots across the cloud with Cloud Auto-Discovery.
Build a complete and dynamic sensitive data inventory with contextual attributes for holistic understanding.
Tailor policies that surface alerts and automatically trigger remediation actions across the right people and tools whenever biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Discovery of biometric identifiers across all NYCHHC systems
dimension_coverage.note: BigID's Hyperscan technology explicitly scans unstructured biometric data across all data source types, directly addressing the need to locate all fingerprint and palm-print records before classification can be applied.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Scan unstructured data, including biometric data, 95% faster with BigID's Hyperscan technology. Save time and avoid sensitive data blind spots across the cloud with Cloud Auto-Discovery.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Separate highest-sensitivity classification tier for biometric identifiers
dimension_coverage.note: BigID supports custom classifier creation and distinct sensitivity tier assignment, enabling NYCHHC to establish biometric fields as a standalone Tier 0 category separate from other PHI tiers.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Customize and fine-tune classifiers to find unique types of biometric data that you may be storing. Label and tag data using a unified ruleset.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Board-level approval workflow for third-party access provisioning to biometric systems
dimension_coverage.note: BigID can trigger approval workflow alerts when biometric data is accessed, but the specific board-level approval routing and governance structure must be configured by NYCHHC's IAM and governance teams.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Tailor policies that surface alerts and automatically trigger remediation actions across the right people and tools whenever biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed.

mitigation_limits.limit: BigID does not itself constitute the board-level governance committee required to approve third-party biometric access; the customer must establish that governance structure and configure BigID's approval workflows to route to the designated board-level delegate.
grounding_spans: Tailor policies that surface alerts and automatically trigger remediation actions across the right people and tools whenever biometric data is stored, shared, or accessed.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: BigID's verified capabilities map directly to biometric data discovery, classification, access-rights remediation, and HIPAA/NIST alignment—closely matching the DAT controls and breach scenario—but scores are moderated by evidence resting entirely on vendor product-page claims with no named customer case studies, zero verified certifications, and a reputation-halo risk given BigID's market prominence in data intelligence.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: BigID
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: BigID Data Intelligence Platform – Healthcare Data Security
risk_coverage_vendors.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: BigID provides an ML-based data intelligence platform that enables healthcare organisations to discover, classify, and monitor access to PHI including biometric data, and to operationalise incident response workflows identifying exactly whose data was impacted in a breach.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: BigID's file-access monitoring and incident response capabilities directly support MC-DAT-016 by providing the detection layer that identifies bulk-access and overly permissive access events on PHI data stores. Its post-breach incident response feature — enabling precise identification of whose data was impacted — addresses the MC-DAT-024 requirement to distinguish biometric breach victims from credential-rotation remediation pathways.

capability_claims.claim: BigID provides high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets, identifies users with access to large, sensitive datasets for investigation, and remediates overexposed data to reduce risk and protect personal information across enterprise data stores.
capability_claims.source_span: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.

capability_claims.claim: BigID simplifies incident response by enabling healthcare organisations to quickly and accurately determine whose data was impacted by a data incident — loss, theft, or misuse — and identify which personal and critical data sets were affected.
capability_claims.source_span: With BigID, users can quickly and accurately understand whose data was impacted by a data incident — loss, theft, or misuse — and identify which personal and critical data sets have been affected.

framework_alignments.source_span: BigID takes an ML-based approach to automatically classify and tag all sensitive, regulated, and high-risk data — by regulation, document type, policy, attributes, person, and more.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: BigID's file-access monitoring module provides permission-level visibility over PHI data stores, identifying accounts — including vendor accounts — with access to large sensitive datasets and surfacing overexposed data for remediation. When a breach occurs, BigID's incident response feature accelerates the identification of exactly which individuals' biometric and medical records were accessed, enabling the healthcare organisation to distinguish affected biometric-data subjects (who require lifetime fraud monitoring) from affected credential holders (who can rotate credentials). This directly supports both the DLP layer required by MC-DAT-016 and the biometric-specific incident response protocol required by MC-DAT-024.
grounding_spans: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.

enabling_features.source_span: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Enables rapid, accurate determination of whose data was impacted in a breach and which specific sensitive datasets were affected, replacing manual review of large data stores with automated analysis.
enabling_features.source_span: With BigID, users can quickly and accurately understand whose data was impacted by a data incident — loss, theft, or misuse — and identify which personal and critical data sets have been affected.

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Configure PHI and biometric data store monitoring: Set up BigID's file-access monitoring across all NYCHHC data stores containing PHI, with specific attention to systems holding biometric fingerprint and palm-print fields.
2. Baseline normal access patterns: Establish per-data-store and per-account access baselines, identifying expected access volumes and user sets for each sensitive data category.
3. Flag overexposed data: Use BigID's permission analysis to identify data stores accessible to third-party vendor accounts beyond contracted scope, and remediate overly broad access before a bulk-copy event can occur.
4. Monitor for high-volume access: Review access logs regularly to identify any vendor account or internal user accessing large sensitive datasets outside normal patterns.
5. Post-breach impact assessment: Following detection of a breach event, run BigID's incident response workflow to rapidly identify all individuals whose biometric, medical, and financial records were in the accessed dataset — enabling segmentation between biometric-data subjects and credential-only-affected individuals.
6. Biometric victim notification routing: Use the impact assessment output to route the 1.8 million biometric-data subjects to the lifetime biometric monitoring pathway rather than the standard credit-monitoring pathway.
grounding_spans: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.
With BigID, users can quickly and accurately understand whose data was impacted by a data incident — loss, theft, or misuse — and identify which personal and critical data sets have been affected.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Detection of bulk file-copy operations on PHI egress points
dimension_coverage.note: BigID monitors file-access permissions and identifies overexposed data stores, but does not function as a real-time DLP egress monitor generating Priority 1 alerts within one hour of a bulk-copy event; dedicated DLP tooling is required for that function.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.

dimension_coverage.dimension: Post-breach identification of biometric-specific breach victims
dimension_coverage.note: BigID's incident response workflow directly addresses this dimension by rapidly identifying whose biometric data was impacted, enabling the routing of affected individuals to biometric-specific monitoring.
dimension_coverage.grounding_span: With BigID, users can quickly and accurately understand whose data was impacted by a data incident — loss, theft, or misuse — and identify which personal and critical data sets have been affected.

mitigation_limits.limit: BigID's file-access monitoring is not a real-time network DLP solution; it provides permission-analysis and post-access visibility rather than inline blocking of bulk-copy sessions. Dedicated network DLP tooling is required to generate real-time egress alerts for the 77-day bulk-copy pattern in the NYCHHC incident.
grounding_spans: Get high-level permission analysis around targeted datasets based on category and type, and identify users with access to large, sensitive datasets for further investigation.

risk_coverage_vendors.score_rationale: BigID's verified capabilities map directly to the claimed controls (PHI discovery, access analysis, incident scoping) and the breach scenario (biometric/PHI exfiltration, identifying impacted individuals), but evidence rests on product-page claims rather than named customer case studies, and no certifications are verified, limiting confidence beyond INFERRED.

risk_coverage_vendors.name: Delinea
risk_coverage_vendors.product_name: Delinea PAM for Healthcare Organizations
risk_coverage_vendors.what_they_do: Delinea provides a privileged access management platform purpose-built for healthcare organisations that enforces just-in-time privilege elevation, session monitoring, role-based access controls, and MFA for all third-party vendor accounts with access to ePHI systems.
risk_coverage_vendors.fit_rationale: Delinea's healthcare PAM product explicitly addresses the replacement of persistent vendor access with least-privilege, session-monitored, MFA-enforced access — directly matching MC-CYB-028's requirement to replace persistent third-party vendor accounts with just-in-time PAM sessions. The platform's documented capability to manage and monitor third-party vendor remote access specifically, and to restrict access via role-based controls, also addresses the network-scope restriction requirement of MC-CYB-015.

capability_claims.claim: Delinea PAM for Healthcare manages and secures remote access to protected healthcare information and it is essential to monitor and report on every privileged session, including users outside the network and vendors, with a revolving door of contractors expanding the attack surface.
capability_claims.source_span: PAM manages and secures remote access to protected healthcare information across the extended organization. It is essential to monitor and report on every privileged sessions, including users outside your network and vendors.

capability_claims.claim: Delinea enforces the principle of least privilege to prevent credential harvesting and lateral movement by a bad actor who exploits entry-level privileges, using a least-standing privilege approach to securing privileged access that enables detection and blocking of further cyberattack attempts.
capability_claims.source_span: PAM enforces the principle of least privilege to prevent credential harvesting and lateral movement by a bad actor who exploits entry-level privileges.

capability_claims.claim: Delinea's built-in MFA checks identities at all major access gates — including login and privilege elevation — so that even if credentials are stolen or shared, unauthorized users cannot leverage them for privileged access entry to healthcare systems.
capability_claims.source_span: With Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), you can tie identities directly to privileged account use, so even if credentials are stolen or shared, unauthorized users can't leverage them for privileged access entry to your healthcare systems.

capability_claims.claim: A Delinea-commissioned case study identified that a healthcare organisation needed a solution that could automate service account management and provide secure but appropriately limited third-party access to systems.
capability_claims.source_span: We needed a solution that could automate the management of our service accounts and also provide secure — but appropriately limited — third-party access to systems.

capability_claims.source_span: Server Pam enables humans and machines to seamlessly authenticate with passwordless login, enforce least privilege with just-in-time privilege elevation

framework_alignments.clause: HIPAA role-based access and privileged session monitoring requirements
framework_alignments.source_span: HIPAA mandates underscore the importance of PAM to ensure healthcare data is private and secure.

mitigation_mechanism.summary: Delinea replaces persistent third-party vendor credentials with just-in-time PAM sessions that require MFA at each elevation, automatically expire after the session window, and produce full session logs retained for audit. For NYCHHC, this means the unnamed vendor's compromised credential — which persisted undetected for 77 days — would instead have been a time-bounded session revoked at expiry, preventing dwell-time accumulation. Delinea also enforces least-standing privilege at the server level via Server PAM, preventing lateral movement across NYCHHC's 70-location network even if a single vendor credential was compromised.
grounding_spans: PAM manages and secures remote access to protected healthcare information across the extended organization. It is essential to monitor and report on every privileged sessions, including users outside your network and vendors.
PAM enforces the principle of least privilege to prevent credential harvesting and lateral movement by a bad actor who exploits entry-level privileges.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Monitors and records every privileged session by vendors and outside-network users, creating an auditable log of all access to ePHI systems that would have detected the NYCHHC bulk-copy behaviour.
enabling_features.source_span: It is essential to monitor and report on every privileged sessions, including users outside your network and vendors.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Checks identity at login and privilege elevation via built-in MFA, so that stolen or shared vendor credentials cannot be leveraged for access without additional authentication.
enabling_features.source_span: With Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), you can tie identities directly to privileged account use, so even if credentials are stolen or shared, unauthorized users can't leverage them for privileged access entry to your healthcare systems.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Prevents credential harvesting and lateral movement by enforcing the minimum privilege required for each vendor's contracted function, blocking the cross-hospital traversal that occurred in the NYCHHC incident.
enabling_features.source_span: PAM enforces the principle of least privilege to prevent credential harvesting and lateral movement by a bad actor who exploits entry-level privileges.

enabling_features.what_it_does: Provides pre-built HIPAA-focused PAM policy statements covering role-based access and privileged session monitoring, accelerating compliant deployment in healthcare environments.
enabling_features.source_span: Get started creating HIPAA-focused PAM policies for your organization with 40+ pre-written policy statements

enabling_features.what_it_does: Grants privilege only at the moment of need and revokes it automatically at session expiry, replacing persistent vendor access with time-bounded sessions and eliminating the standing privilege that enabled the 77-day NYCHHC dwell-time.
enabling_features.source_span: Server Pam enables humans and machines to seamlessly authenticate with passwordless login, enforce least privilege with just-in-time privilege elevation

workflow_steps.steps:
1. Vendor account inventory: Use Delinea's Secret Server to discover and vault all existing third-party vendor credentials with access to NYCHHC systems, producing a complete inventory of persistent accounts.
2. Replace persistent with JIT: Convert all vendor accounts from persistent access to just-in-time PAM sessions via Server PAM — setting a maximum session window (e.g. 8 hours) and configuring automatic revocation at session expiry.
3. Enforce MFA at elevation: Require Delinea's built-in MFA at both login and privilege elevation for every vendor session, ensuring stolen credentials cannot be used without the second factor.
4. Scope network access: Assign each vendor account to a specific network zone covering only the systems required for the contracted service function, enforcing least-standing privilege to prevent lateral movement across NYCHHC's 70-location network.
5. Monitor and record all vendor sessions: Activate full session recording for all vendor privileged sessions, retaining logs for a minimum of 12 months for forensic and audit purposes.
6. Alert on anomalous session behaviour: Configure Delinea's session monitoring to flag sessions exhibiting unusual duration, off-hours access, or access to systems outside the vendor's contracted scope, escalating to the SOC for investigation.
grounding_spans: PAM manages and secures remote access to protected healthcare information across the extended organization. It is essential to monitor and report on every privileged sessions, including users outside your network and vendors.
PAM enforces the principle of least privilege to prevent credential harvesting and lateral movement by a bad actor who exploits entry-level privileges.
